column1: Text

As the 20th century dawned, the proliferation of automobiles, exemplified by Ford's Model T, revolutionized transportation. However, contemporary discourse increasingly questions our reliance on these vehicles, and personally, I concur with the sentiment that a reduction in car usage is warranted.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" serves as a compelling illustration of this concern. Experts posit that the ubiquity of cars, characteristic of suburban environments from Shanghai to Chicago, significantly impedes efforts to mitigate vehicle pollution. Astonishingly, while cars contribute to 12% of Europe's pollution, this figure escalates to a staggering 50% in the US due to our extensive dependence on automobiles! Moreover, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," underscores the detrimental environmental impact of car usage, exemplified by Paris's imposition of driving bans based on license plates to combat smog.

In contrast, Andrew Selsky's piece, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," presents an encouraging alternative. Millions of Colombians participated in a car-free day, opting for walking, cycling, or public transportation, resulting in remarkably tranquil streets. This annual event, which restricts private vehicle usage while permitting buses and taxis, has yielded notable improvements in air quality. Furthermore, Bogota has undergone a transformation, with the emergence of new parks, sports facilities, sidewalks, and commercial establishments.

In conclusion, the reduction of car usage and the implementation of car-free initiatives offer substantial benefits, including improved air quality and urban environments. The success of Bogota's car-free day demonstrates the feasibility of alleviating traffic congestion and enhancing urban living. By adopting similar strategies in America, such as prioritizing cycling and walking for short distances, we could foster a more sustainable future. Indeed, advocating for a reduction in car usage represents a prudent and advantageous course of action.

Every country on Earth needs transportation. It helps people get around faster using cars and trains. But, using cars so much causes a big problem: lots of pollution. Many of us drive a lot, and this makes the air dirty. So, we need to find ways to make less pollution.

Think about Vauban, a small town in Germany. People there don't use cars much. They live close to things they need, like stores. This makes it easy to get around. Cars aren't completely gone, but most people in Vauban don't own one. Surprisingly, more than half of families gave up their cars to live there. They said they felt less stressed because they didn't have to drive as much. Now, cars cause 12% of the world's pollution. But in places where people drive a lot, like many parts of the United States, it can be as high as 50%.

Paris had a lot of smog, so they tried something new. On Mondays, cars with license plates ending in even numbers couldn't be driven. If they did, they got a big fine. The next day, it was the opposite: odd-numbered plates were banned. This quickly cut down traffic by 60% in just five days. Diesel cars, which are common in France, made the smog problem worse. But, after they stopped the alternating ban, it didn't work as well anymore.

Imagine a day with no cars. This happens every year in Bogota, Colombia. Only taxis and buses are allowed. This one day makes a big difference in pollution. This has been happening since the 1990s. After that, they built bike paths, parks, and places for people to hang out. It's hard to live without cars, but it's good for the environment.

We probably can't get rid of cars completely anytime soon. But countries should try to make less pollution and encourage people to drive less. If we do things to help with air pollution, it will make a real difference. We use cars a lot, but using them too much hurts the environment and us.

Elisabeth Rosenthal is curious: Is America starting to like cars less? Her main point? Car use is decreasing, which could be fantastic! Imagine: fewer wrecks, healthier folks, and cleaner air thanks to less driving. Maybe we just don't need cars as much anymore.

For starters, driving less could seriously boost our health and safety – crucial stuff, right? Andrew Selsky recalled how calm and pleasant the streets were in Colombia on a car-free day. Fewer cars equals less stress! Traffic jams ruin our plans and frustrate us. So, if we drive less, we could have less traffic and feel less stressed! Heidrun Walter said she felt happier and less stressed after getting rid of her car. Plus, we’d exercise more. Sure, cars are convenient, but we’re so busy we forget to move. Selsky saw tons of new parks and sports areas. Less cars could lead to more parks, which then encourages exercise. Basically, driving less means less stress and a healthier life!

Also, driving less helps the planet. Maybe not everyone cares about the environment, but it’s important. Pollution hurts everything, and cars pollute a lot. Rosenthal says that less driving "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving, less pollution – a chance for a brighter future. Pollution is a major problem, and drivers add to it.

Also, we would rely on cars less. Many think they NEED them, but are they just addicted? Technology has changed us, and we're losing touch with nature. Being too dependent isn't good. David Goldberg says that "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some might disagree, but depending on something harmful isn’t progress. Rosenthal points out that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even buy a car. People who rely on cars have forgotten about nature and don’t always think about the environment. Less driving could solve that.

Basically, driving less could mean better health, less pollution, and less reliance on cars. It's a cleaner, safer world for everyone. Maybe America is getting better by driving less and thinking about what’s good for everyone.

Do you ever just drive, and drive, and drive? Maybe to work, or maybe just to the store? Ever stop and think about how much driving everyone does, even for short trips? It’s super easy to not even consider how all this driving is messing with the planet. Could you maybe skip driving today? People always shout, “But I need my car!” However, it's totally possible to cut back. Places like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have already proved it. Honestly, driving less is a win-win for you and everyone else.

Believe it or not, driving less can actually boost your mood! Heidrun Walter said that people feel way less stressed when they ditch the car. Just think about how awful road rage is. Driving is a hassle – figuring out directions, dealing with jerks on the road – it all adds up to stress. Big cities like New York and Paris are notorious for crazy traffic and nasty air. Robert Duffer, in his article "Paris bans driving due to smog," pointed out how warm air traps all the fumes from cars, which is seriously bad news. Duffer also mentioned that Paris temporarily banned cars because of terrible pollution, and the air cleared up super fast! Instead of driving, people could walk, bike, or even skate. It's way better for you, and you'll feel much happier than if you were stuck in a car.

Cutting back on cars can make cities way more beautiful. Pollution is a bummer for plants and stinks, making everything depressing. Less driving means fewer nasty fumes and cleaner cities. Elisabeth Rosenthal, in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars," explained that fewer cars mean more people walking, which leads to more sidewalks and cool shops. Andrew Selsky mentioned that they added parks, sports areas, nice sidewalks, new restaurants, and awesome stores. The EPA is a big fan of areas with fewer cars, as Rosenthal noted, because it seriously cuts down on pollution. Bogotá, Colombia, even has a "car-free" day every year and fines drivers who break the rules. Even though fining people can be a bit of a hassle, it actually works, and smog is reduced! So, bottom line: driving less is good for your health, creates better cities, and helps make the world cleaner.

It’s easy to see why cars are so popular, but they matter more than we think. They get us from place to place quickly. Sure, there are trains and buses, but cars seem better to many. Yet, too many cars cause pollution and stress. Taking buses or trains means fewer expensive repairs, less traffic, and less pollution. So, using cars less is a good idea.

Cutting back on cars helps us and the planet, mainly by cleaning up the air. Cars' engines release bad gases. One car might not seem like much, but tons of cars, like in big cities like Paris, are a huge problem. Paris has even banned cars because of pollution. Robert Duffer wrote in "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog" that cars with license plates ending in even numbers were not allowed to drive for a while and had to pay a fine if they did, and then odd numbers. This cut traffic by 60%! Andrew Selsky wrote in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" that Bogota, Colombia, has car-free days to clean the air, get people to use other ways to get around, and help the environment. The world sees that using cars less helps the environment.

Also, think about less stress! Driving is stressful because of traffic, car problems, and noise. Less driving means less stress. No traffic, repair bills, or loud noises make life calmer. Elisabeth Rosenthal quoted Hartmut Walter in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," who said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." This shows that not having a car lowers stress. Andrew Selsky also said in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" that car-free days are "a good opportunity to take away stress..." It's smart to focus on peace of mind because less stress makes life better.

In the end, using cars less is good for the earth and us. Our planet needs help, and stress is bad for us. If we find ways to use cars less, we can help the environment and lower our stress. It might be hard at first, but many places are doing it, and it works. It makes for a better, more balanced life.

The Electoral College is weird, right? Instead of us choosing the president directly, we choose people who then choose the president for us, be they Democrat or Republican. But guess what? Source two says electors can ignore our wishes! Is that even fair? We vote expecting someone good to improve things, but this complicates it. The worst part? That "winner-take-all" rule.

We don't directly elect the president now. As source two notes, we elect electors. Each state gets a set number. These electors then decide who wins. Source two says there are 538 electors; 270 wins. A state's elector count is based on its congressional representation. If we want fairness, every vote must matter! Scrapping the Electoral College ensures every vote counts. Candidates must earn votes with actual support, and ties become rare. Now, if most people in your state want someone else, your vote is meaningless. Ditch the college, and BAM, your vote counts!

Source two mentions "faithless" electors, meaning some might vote for the other candidate! This is a national insult. A Republican elector voting Democrat negates all Republican votes. We elect a president every four years. Electors changing their vote? Absolutely not.

Sources two and three emphasize the "winner-take-all" rule. It's harsh. Losing means your votes are worthless. The winner gets everything! It's as if all the loser's voters secretly wanted the winner.

Senators, abolish the Electoral College! It's a flawed system. It values some votes more than others. It lets electors vote however they want. And the "winner-take-all" rule? It discards votes for those who lost.

Esteemed Senator,

I'm writing about a crucial change needed in how we pick our president. It's really important that we think about getting rid of the Electoral College. We need a system that shows what most people want more fairly. Right now, this old system is unfair because electors can go against what the public voted for.

I get that changing the Electoral College might seem tough. But, making this change could really help us later on. If we just counted everyone's votes directly, candidates might make a lot of promises just to get votes. Plus, we have to think about what could happen if there's a tie and how to make sure everything's fair. So, even though totally ditching the Electoral College might not be best, fixing what's wrong with it is a must.

Right now, electors have way too much say in who wins the presidency. The "winner-takes-all" thing in the Electoral College hurts voters. Candidates spend too much time in a few "swing" states and ignore the others. Think about 2000 when seventeen states didn't get much attention. Because of problems like these, the Electoral College is old-fashioned and needs to be fixed or gotten rid of.

Candidates pick electors in a smart way to help their chances. These electors help get support without the candidate having to go to every state. Basically, the candidate just waits to see what happens. A candidate could win without trying too hard because electors decide who wins in the end. This lets candidates possibly bribe electors in important states.

When people vote, they're not really voting for the candidates themselves. They're voting for electors. Electors get chosen in different ways, like at state meetings or by the candidates. A big problem with the Electoral College is that something really bad could happen unexpectedly. Like in 1960, some people in Louisiana thought about changing Democratic electors to ones against John F. Kennedy.

To wrap it up, the Electoral College is an old way to run elections. It gives too much power to electors instead of the people. It might have made sense a long time ago, but it doesn't work for our democracy now.

Okay, everyone, pay attention! Cars are everywhere, right? Easy to use, sure. But what if we didn't use them so much? Just picture it: cleaner air and more money in your pocket. Seriously, driving less is a good idea.

Take Paris, as an example. Robert Duffer said, "Paris bans driving due to smog." What they did was pretty clever. One day, cars with odd license plate numbers were allowed; the next day, it was even numbers. Break the rules, and you got a fine! They even made buses and trains free for a week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air became cleaner, and people saved money. Duffer noted that traffic jams fell by 60%! He explained, "Traffic jams dropped 60 percent in Paris, after five days of really bad smog… almost as bad as Beijing, China, which is known for being super polluted." Even though the smog was bad, fewer cars made a HUGE impact.

Also, look at Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all out, practically getting rid of cars entirely. About 70% don't even own one because parking is expensive outside of town, and cars aren't usually allowed. They love it there. Heidrun Walter stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Not many young folks bother getting their license! Rosenthal wrote in "The end of car culture" that cars are just tools to get around. No one cares much about the car itself, just getting where they need to go easily.

The point is, cars are helpful, but so are buses, trains, bikes, and sharing rides. Some of these are even free! Plus, less pollution and traffic? That’s a win-win for our health and the planet.

Plumer thinks the Electoral College is just plain wrong. Source 2 says it's old and doesn't make sense. Lots of people agree – they think it's either a disaster waiting to happen or just a bad idea in general. Sure, some say it stops ties or helps bigger states, but a lot of folks don't buy it. They just want a simple popular vote; they think it's way better.

One big problem is that some votes count more than others. Electors have way more power than regular people. If we voted directly, every single vote would matter the same. Plus, it's scary that electors could be swayed or bought off. That messes up the whole point of democracy.

A lot of people think the Electoral College is a relic from the past. Plumer (Source 2) even quotes Bob Dole saying, "Get rid of the Electoral College!" Lots of people feel that way. They want to vote directly instead of having a few people decide. Some joke that a kid throwing darts could pick a better president. Plumer (Source 2) points out that "More than 60 percent of voters want a direct election," and even more feel that way since 2000.

Seriously, the Electoral College is weird. It's this confusing thing where electors, not us, pick the president. Plumer (Source 2) says, "You vote for electors, then they vote for the president. Who are these electors? Usually just normal people. Who picks them? It depends on the state – maybe a party, maybe the candidate. Can we control them? Not always! Do people mess up and vote for the wrong ones? Sometimes!" It makes you wonder who's really in charge.

Maybe the Electoral College has some good points, like keeping things stable, but most people think it's not worth it. A simple popular vote seems much fairer. Like Plumer (Source 2) says, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

From our nation's beginnings, the Electoral College was made a part of the Constitution. I think we ought to keep it because, up until now, it's worked well, so there is no reason to change it. Keeping the Electoral College is a solid idea, because it helps us in about five main ways.

Okay, so the Electoral College has some drawbacks. Like, electors *could* ditch their party and vote however. We don't actually vote for the president; we vote for electors, and they pick the president. They could totally ignore the popular vote and do what they want. Also, a state could send in two different lists of electors, which would be a total disaster. And even though ties aren't common, the Electoral College always carries that risk. Because of the "winner-takes-all" rule, candidates often ignore some states because one party is way ahead. With a direct vote, they might campaign harder everywhere to get more votes. But, criticisms usually point to weird scenarios; therefore, it's smart to look at things differently.

Swing states call the shots in presidential elections, so their voters are usually more informed. Since they know their votes matter, they pay close attention to candidates and the news. Candidates spend lots of time and money trying to win them over. Many other states are basically decided before voting even starts, because one party is so strong. Maybe voters in swing states think more about who they like and what they believe in; maybe they are the best voters to elect our president.

The Electoral College keeps us from needing extra elections, which lowers confusion. Without it, a really close direct vote could cause big problems. The Electoral College's value can be seen through Nixon's win in '68 and Clinton's in '92; they won with about 43% of the votes.

I get why some voters in states strongly tied to one side might dislike the Electoral College. Since their votes can feel useless, they might skip campaigns. One person's vote doesn't matter much alone, and in 2012, some people didn't even vote. For some, just saying who they like is more important than changing the election. If you are involved in government, your vote is more likely to matter.

Senator,

Look, the Electoral College needs to stay! It has 538 people. To win, a candidate needs more than 270 votes. States pick these electors, usually linked to a candidate. It’s vital to keep this system. It ensures a clear winner for everyone.

A key reason to keep it? It almost always picks a clear victor. A tie *could* occur, yet it’s rare, even with the current number of electors. In 2012, Obama won about 62% of electoral votes, but just 51% of the popular vote. Most states have a winner-take-all setup. Even a slim lead can mean a huge electoral win. However, this system leads people to mostly chase votes in swing states, while neglecting others. Still, the winner often gets more electoral votes than actual votes.

Furthermore, the President represents *everyone*. The Electoral College forces candidates to get votes nationwide. No single area has enough votes to decide the president. Even with strong local support, you still need to campaign elsewhere. More votes in your easy states don't get more electoral votes. A president lacking broad support? That's not good. People might feel ignored.

Ultimately, we *must* keep the Electoral College. It prevents ties and guarantees the president serves *all* Americans.

Heidrun Walter once felt stressed by car ownership. Now, she loves the freedom of life without a car! This mirrors a growing trend: more people ditching car dependence. Cars *used* to be seen as vital, but falling sales and less driving show people are waking up to the upside of reducing car use. This switch could seriously help curb the greenhouse effect, cut smog and pollution, and boost different parts of the economy, not just cars.

The greenhouse effect, worsened by car fumes, hurts nature and people. The article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" says cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, and almost 50% in some US spots! Less driving means fewer emissions, which is great for the planet. This, in turn, could help with big problems like global warming and rising seas. So, less car use equals fewer greenhouse gases.

Smog and air pollution are serious threats, hurting our health and the environment. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," reports, "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This ban improved air quality in those five days. More actions like this could cut pollution and smog. It might also nudge people to try buses, bikes, or walking. "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" shows how banning cars can lead to more bike paths, parks, sports centers, better sidewalks, and smoother traffic. Therefore, using cars less can result in improved urban living.

Lastly, cutting car use can give the economy a boost in several ways. One source pointed out that lots of US transport money goes to highways. If we needed cars less, that money could go to schools, government programs, and local projects. Also, as another source says, using cars less saves resources, letting people spend money elsewhere. The drop in driving proves this change. One source noted, "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This frees up focus on economic improvement. In other words, less car use means economic wins, like more government spending, personal savings, and growth.

So, cutting car use has tons of good sides. It can fire up the economy, stop greenhouse gases, and ease smog and pollution in crowded cities.

Picking a president? Huge deal. What they do can change everything for better or worse, really. Now, the Electoral College, it tries to get a feel for what each state wants. But, does it work? That's up for debate. I think it's pretty good at showing where each state stands and, like, what the country's feeling.

People slam the Electoral College, saying it doesn't pick the best person or it's just not fair. Still, we get to CHOOSE! Our opinions matter. Every vote counts big time – way better than not voting at all. It's not perfect, sure, but nothing ever is. So, yeah, I think the Electoral College does a decent job of adding up the votes.

Trying to find a better way is tough. The big worry is that the Electoral College doesn't show what everyone really wants. But I don't buy it. When we vote, we're trying to get our person elected. Those votes help steer the Electoral College. And it gets states working together, since the most votes show what each state wants, which I dig.

Some complain that bigger states have too much power in the Electoral College. Maybe they're right, but it kind of makes sense. States with more people should get more votes, right? Still, every single vote counts the same, so it's not like they totally rule. Even if people disagree, the Electoral College gives us a fair way to see what the country's thinking, which is what we want.

Basically, the Electoral College does a solid job of figuring out what each state wants by using the most votes to decide who wins. It's not perfect, but just adding up individual votes might be worse. All systems have problems, but the Electoral College is a pretty organized way to count votes over time. Not everyone will love it, because you can't please everyone!

Dear Senator, So, I've been thinking about the best way to choose our President and their team, and I've landed on something. Basically, there are two options: the Electoral College, or just counting up all the votes. After thinking it over, one seems way better to me.

With the Electoral College, people called electors vote for the President and Vice President. These 538 people decide who wins. Honestly, I don't like this setup. We think we're voting for the president directly, but we're really just picking electors who then make the call. Why should a small group, compared to millions of voters, have so much power in picking our leader, leaving us all waiting to see what they decide? It feels unfair that their opinions matter so much more than regular people's when it comes to deciding who runs the country.

On the other hand, a popular vote system would let every person vote directly for who they want as president and their team. This way, we can clearly say who we want to lead us, hoping for good changes. Even if the candidate loses, your vote still matters because it adds to the nation's voice. The Electoral College might be faster, but knowing your vote really counts could get more people involved and interested in the candidates and what they stand for. We get to help shape our future, instead of just hoping an elector agrees with us.

I know the Electoral College has some good points, like making sure there's a clear winner. Some might say that picking electors is the same as voting directly. But, these small perks don't matter as much as letting everyone vote directly, which is a basic democratic right.

Basically, when we think about how to pick the president, we have to ask: How do you want your voice to be heard? Do you want to be directly involved, or are you okay with letting others decide for you? For me, it's clear: I want the system that makes our democracy louder.

Picture a nice family walk in the park, but darn, pollution makes it tough! We sure do love our cars way too much. We should take buses, walk, or even carpool sometimes. Driving less is a big deal, ya know? Cars cost a lot, keep us from using other ways to get around, and hurt Mother Earth.

Unexpected bills pop up when you own a car! In Vauban, Germany, a parking spot can cost a whopping $40,000 – more than some houses! In Bogota, Columbia, if you drive on the wrong day, bam! $25 fine. Driving in Paris? That might cost you 22 euros because of your license plate. And gas? Always expensive! Rich folks might not care, but families with kids? Big ouch!

People often forget how awesome public transport is. Just think, no buses, trains, or subways...try driving into New York City then! Bogota makes folks take the bus or walk by fining drivers on car-free days. The mayor, Antanas Mockus, was super happy with how many people joined in, even in the rain! Other cities like Cali and Valledupar copied them, and everyone was interested. New York even has bikes to share. Despite some doubts, public transport is super important for saving the planet.

Ugh, smog hides the stars sometimes. Paris even said "no cars for a bit" because of too much yuck in the air, and it got 60% better in five days! But France likes diesel fuel, which is a big no-no for pollution, so it's complicated. Bogota's car-free day gives us a break from smog, and Vauban barely uses cars, so less smog there too! Even tiny changes make a big difference for the planet.

Vauban and Bogota show us that less driving means less pollution. Paris bans cars to fight smog. Vauban makes parking super pricey, so folks don't drive as much. Bogota fines people, so they walk or take the bus. We gotta stop using cars so much because it saves money, makes us use other ways to get around, and cleans up the air.

Less driving is good for our town! All over the world, places like France and Colombia push for other ways to get around. People feel less stressed when they don't have to drive everywhere. Even studies here show fewer people own cars. Now is the perfect time to try new ideas, like those in Vauban or Bogota, and stop wasting time stuck in traffic.

Think about this: one neighborhood in Germany shows we can live without cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal pointed out that Vauban, Germany is mostly "car-free." Most people there like using public transportation. The article also said, "70% of families don't own cars, and lots of people sell their cars when they move there!" One person living there said they feel calmer and happier without a car because they don't have the stress of driving. Lots of places want to be like this, cutting down on cars to help the planet. Expect more "smart planning" to come. If we all work together to drive less, our town can become a car-free dream.

Bogota, Colombia has car-free days to get people to try other ways of getting around and make the air cleaner. When people visit for the first time, they're always amazed at how much better the air is. Choosing a "car-free" life helps people be more active and makes the city look nicer. Reports show more parks and sports fields, wide and clean sidewalks, and cool shops and restaurants. It's not just cleaner air; it makes the city a more fun and pretty place to be!

Picture using ideas like they have in Bogota and Vauban. Fewer people are buying cars around here already. Some folks are even skipping driver's ed. One writer noticed that Americans aren't as crazy about cars anymore. Another said we hit peak mileage a while back, and it's been going down since. Young people are a big part of this change. They use social media, phones, carpools, and buses more, so they drive less. Car companies might not like it, but many of us think we'll have better and easier ways to travel because of it.

In the end, less driving means less pollution and stress, nicer neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are trying to cut down on cars to fight pollution. If we go green and car-free, we'll be part of a global change!

Esteemed Senator, I'm writing to insist that we get rid of the Electoral College. I really think the person who becomes president should only be decided by the total number of votes from the whole country. Honestly, I see the Electoral College as super unfair and a big threat to what the U.S. Constitution is all about.

The Electoral College messes up the whole idea of what makes our country a democracy. We vote, sure, but the Electoral College can change what we all decided, which feels so wrong! It makes you wonder if we're really a democracy. Think about the 2000 election: Gore won the popular vote, but Bush won because of the Electoral College. Like "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "More than 60% of voters want a normal election, not this thing." People clearly want a simple way to vote.

Also, I really don't like the Electoral College because it could hurt the U.S. Constitution, especially our important rights. The main thing is that we, the people, pick our president. But when the person with the most votes loses because of the Electoral College, it's bad for democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" worries about weird elections, asking: "What if a state sends in two sets of electors? It happened in Hawaii in '60. Luckily, Nixon just used the electors for his opponent." Imagine the chaos if that happened again and no one stopped it, giving someone a win they didn't deserve! The Electoral College is like a disaster waiting to happen.

So, let's get rid of the Electoral College! It's just not fair and could really mess up our U.S. Constitution. I hope you think about this when you're making decisions about this important issue.

Air pollution from how people get around is a really big problem all over the place. Lots of folks probably don't get just how much better the environment could be if we all drove our cars less. Cars are always pumping out nasty stuff. So, really think hard before you buy a car, because the bad stuff for the environment is way worse than just having a car.

Think about cars, buses, and those big trucks. They guzzle gas like crazy, which is super bad for the earth. The stuff we burn is messing up the whole planet! If we keep going like this, we could wreck the ozone layer, and then we'd have nowhere to live! Plus, people use cars way too much. They drive even when they could walk or bike, which makes things even worse. Did you hear about Paris? They made it harder to drive when the air was bad, and traffic dropped like crazy – like 60% in just five days! Seriously, the way we get around is a real "death wish" for the environment.

Buying a car? Get ready for a ton of costs you don't even think about at first. In Vauban, Germany, check this out: you HAVE to buy a parking spot to even own a car! And those spots can cost like $40,000 – on top of what you paid for your house! And don't forget the crazy price of gas. Car insurance is just another kick in the wallet. Oh, and driving is often scary, stressful, and just plain not fun.

Automobiles? Everywhere! They shape how we live today. Getting rid of them? A major victory for Earth! Too many cars? Think worse pollution and gross, unhealthy air.

Bogota, Colombia did a "No Car Day"—a cool idea worth trying elsewhere. People used their legs, bikes, and buses instead of cars, which really cleared up the city! Light rain? Didn't matter! Most joined in anyway. The mayor said the weather wasn't a big deal. The aim? Better air, thanks to other ways to get around. But doing this in the U.S. might be hard since we love our cars so much.

Paris fought smog from cars by stopping people from driving when pollution was high. At first, people weren't happy. Reuters said almost 4,000 tickets were given and 27 cars taken away! But traffic dropped a huge 60%! Still, the air was still smoggy like in Beijing. It's not clear if people will accept these rules for good.

Less driving could upset people: harder to get around! HOWEVER, new research says fewer Americans are buying cars, driving, or even getting driver's licenses. A professor at Drexel University thinks this means things are changing. People stopped driving because they had to; maybe they won't go back. Car use in the U.S. might stay down, improving air quality.

Cars everywhere are hurting the environment around the world. Driving less is hard, with lots of problems. But the good things that could happen are clear. Picture cleaner air, more people walking, biking, and skating. Changing our car habits? That makes life better for the environment. Less driving? It helps save the planet!

Whether cutting back on driving does anything relies heavily on where you live and how devoted you are. Worldwide, there are tests going on that look into towns with fewer—or even zero—cars. Places with horrible pollution frequently make a big effort to reduce car usage to clean up the air.

Rosenthal’s piece, "In German Suburb..." paints a vivid picture of life sans automobile. Because the setup is in place, walking and biking become a whole lot simpler. This transition matters quite a bit. Given the pollution problem, giving this a go in other spots seems like a great idea.

Certain cities suffer from unbelievably awful pollution issues. Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving...", highlights the smog in Paris, and how it led to a ban on driving. Folks got angry, so they gave fines to folks who broke the rules and took their cars. Duffer notes that "Congestion was down 60 percent...", showing how useful the ban was to cut back on traffic.

Across the globe, different plans are attempting to get people to use ways to travel that don't cause pollution. Selsky's "Carfree day..." shows how well it worked in Bogota. Millions walked, biked, or skated even when it poured rain. As Selsky says, Bogota also feels more cozy. "Parks…sidewalks" have been upgraded, improving the city and helping everyone.

We must get used to the quick shifts happening around the world. Young adults are starting to wonder if having a car is even needed, picking options that save money and help the planet. The idea that everyone needs a car is losing steam. It is still there, but getting rarer.

The Electoral College is still under fire! Should it stay or go? It's a bit strange, isn't it? Why have a system where we vote for someone who *then* chooses the president? If America is all about citizens choosing the president, shouldn't the person with the most votes win?

When you cast your ballot for president, what's *really* happening? Are you just picking someone who will vote later? Seriously? The article "Does the Electoral College Work?" says we're voting for "your candidate's electors." The Constitution says we vote for our president, but how does that even work? It feels wrong for America and its voting system. And can we even trust these electors? "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "...Can voters actually control who their electors vote for? Not always." So, the person you want might *not* get your vote? The Electoral College might be blocking your direct vote and messing with your right to have your vote count! It's a mess, and your voice should be heard!

Some say the Electoral College is there to make sure we get a president, but does it pick who most people want? Usually, it doesn't. The popular vote often feels pointless, like our voices don't matter. It feels like losing a basic American right. The popular vote should win, not the Electoral College. Al Gore lost even though more people voted for him. "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gore—thanks to the quirks of the electoral college—won the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This shows how it makes our votes worth less. The same article says "...over 60 percent of voters would rather vote directly than have this system." If votes *really* matter, we should vote on whether to keep it.

Some think the Electoral College keeps things running smoothly and stops voting problems. But is that even true? Every system has issues, but the Electoral College isn't the fix. America was built on the idea that we listen to what the people vote for, and I want to see that happen. I want to vote directly for who I want, not vote for someone else to vote.

Basically, the Electoral College is unfair, broken, and un-American. It stops us from voting for who we want and forces us to vote for electors. If you care about American values, you can see how wrong this is. It's a bad way to handle votes, and the American people should be in charge!

Senator,

So, why do we still use the Electoral College? Some claim it ruins democracy because we vote for electors, not the leader we actually want. But it truly functions effectively! It assists in swiftly determining the victor and provides us with a president who represents everyone.

Its purpose is to ensure a decisive victory. Simply voting for the most popular candidate could create problems! Richard A. Posner notes that the winner typically receives considerably more Electoral College votes than popular votes. This reduces the likelihood of disputes over the outcome. He's correct; those votes simplify matters. Additionally, it prevents ties, such as a hypothetical Nixon-Clinton tie in '92. Because it tallies more votes, the Electoral College clarifies the winner.

Additionally, it compels the president to consider all citizens. Candidates require votes everywhere, not just in their strongholds. We need a president for everyone! Imagine Republicans exclusively favoring the South. If a Republican only caters to the South, that's unfair to the rest. Bradford Plumer says candidates disregard states they're unlikely to win. Yet, maybe those states are already decided! Why would a Democrat campaign in Texas, a Republican stronghold? Or a Republican in California, a Democratic stronghold? The voters have likely already made their choice! Old ideas like Plumer's may be outdated. Times change! If you dislike the Electoral College, you need current information. Consider recent elections! Perceptions in 2004, 2008, and 2012 depended on events, promises, and youth turnout. Thus, the Electoral College ensures the president cares about the entire nation.

Therefore, retain the Electoral College! It declares the winner and enables the president to represent us all. Electoral College votes are often higher, simplifying the identification of the victor. Furthermore, it encourages the president to consider the entire country, not just select regions. Senator, please preserve it! It's been effective recently and should continue to be.

Way back when, getting around meant walking. Trips to stores, meals, and seeing family all involved being on foot. But these days, why the car craze? Cities all over are pushing for less driving.

Right from the start, cars aren't kind to our planet. But hey, we're learning! Fresh rules are popping up to cut down on car use. In Vauban, Germany, folks live car-free to give the Earth a break, and guess what? They love it! Heidrun Walter, a mom in Vauban, exclaimed, "Cars just stressed me out. I'm way happier now!"

Paris, the city known for love, kicked cars to the curb because the air was awful. Traffic jams? Down big time—a whopping 60 percent—in just five days!

Because Paris was choking on the worst air in Europe, something had to give. The ban really worked, making the air cleaner and waving goodbye to smog.

Big apples like New York, with their tight streets, get super clogged with cars. Buildings are bunched up, so hoofing it or biking often beats driving. New York's bike setup proves it. Plus, carpooling is getting hot, with people sharing rides to cut down on the car count. That means less gunk in the air, fewer traffic headaches, and more cash saved on gas.

Bill Ford, who's in charge at Ford, dreams of a future where owning a car is a pain. He wants to team up with phone companies to build cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all play nice together, saving time and energy, cutting pollution, and making things safer." Banning cars everywhere isn't the answer since they're often handier than trains or walking, but using them less is a win. Too many cars equals way too much pollution! What if we had a car-free day everywhere and gave other travel a shot? Biking rocks for exercise and is faster than walking, so you get fit and show up sooner!

Presidential elections? The Electoral College makes them a wild ride. Should it stay or go? It mixes people's votes with those from Congress. Past elections had issues, and future ones could be even crazier. Why not directly vote for the president in a democracy? We need something better and should scrap the Electoral College.

Here's the catch: We pick electors, who then pick the president, not us. Even if the popular vote wins, problems remain, like "winner-take-all." "The Indefensible Electoral College" argues this hurts voters. Candidates skip states they can't win, focusing on swing states. They camp out in some states but ignore others. In 2000, 17 states got no visits, showing how flawed the system is.

Another worry is "the disaster factor." Bradford Plumer thinks we were lucky in 2000, but the system could have bigger issues. In Louisiana, some almost replaced Democratic electors with anti-Kennedy people to mess up the election. That shows how messed up things could get. Or, states could send different electors, or we could see a tie. Even with fixes, a better system is needed.

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, saying it keeps things fair. He says it gives smaller states more power to balance things with bigger states. But that's not balance; it favors smaller states. Posner also thinks voters in swing states get more involved. Plumer disagrees, noting campaigns ignored places like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Many voters in big TV markets saw no campaign ads. The Electoral College is easily manipulated and should be scrapped.

I still think the Electoral College should go. People should pick their president. Though it's difficult, the current system is obviously unfair. Some defend the Electoral College, but that's not true. We need a complete overhaul or big changes to the system.

Esteemed Florida State Senator, The Electoral College – quite a topic! Some say it's a solid way to pick presidents, but others think it's just plain unfair and old-fashioned. We need something better to fix these worries. Shouldn't a real democracy have a voting setup that truly shows what it stands for?

Those who like the Electoral College talk about how rare ties are, or how electors usually vote how people want. But come on, just because it doesn't happen often doesn't mean it's okay! Small problems can get bigger, like a hole in your sock. If we don't fix the Electoral College now, we could have bigger issues later. Electors *might* listen to the people, but they could also do their own thing!

The Electoral College? It makes your vote feel less important. You vote, yeah, but electors make the final call. And some states get more electors than they should, which feels… wrong. Like some votes count more. This makes people not want to vote; they feel like they don't matter! Plus, candidates only go to states where they can win electoral votes. Disengaged voters? Yep.

Besides making citizens feel powerless, the Electoral College has other issues. Ties can happen, even if they're rare. Then, the House of Representatives gets involved. This could lead to a choice that's different from what the people wanted. Oh, and you can even win the most votes but still lose the election because of the Electoral College – remember Al Gore in 2000? See? It's flawed. Let's fix it *before* it messes up more elections.

The Electoral College works, kind of, but it doesn't really show what the people want. Electors, *not* us, choose the president. That's a problem for a government that's supposed to be for the people. There's a better way, one that really fits with what democracy is all about. Speak up! Let's get rid of the Electoral College and support a system that truly embodies what America stands for.

Cars are easy to use for travel, but they make a lot of pollution that hurts the Earth. Using cars less is a good fix, and it helps in many ways: it lowers greenhouse gases, cuts down on smog, and saves important stuff.

First off, when we don't use cars as much, we really cut back on greenhouse gases. Think about riding bikes, hopping on trains, or just walking to work. Cars pump out a lot of exhaust that turns into these gases, which is bad news for the environment. One study, let's call it Source 1, says regular cars make about half of all these gases. So, fewer cars on the road means a healthier planet, plain and simple.

Plus, using cars less makes a big dent in smog. Smog is nasty stuff that's bad for nature and for us. Imagine smog as dirty air that makes you sick. Lots of cars can make smog super bad in some places. Source 2 pointed out that Paris almost had worse air than China, and China's air is known to be pretty bad. The air gets way better when we drive less. How do we know? Paris saw smog drop by 60% in just five days when people drove less. So, cleaner air is a huge win from cutting back on cars.

Finally, driving less helps us save vital resources. Source 1 says that if we use cars less, cities might not have to grow as big. This means we wouldn't need as much land for roads, stores, and houses. Source 4 says this would save tons of building materials and protect what nature gives us. To sum it up, less driving equals a better life for everyone.

In short, relying less on cars is good for the Earth, good for our health, and helps save important stuff for the future.

Believe it or not, the Electoral College, though many want it gone, actually has more good than bad. The Constitution set it up on purpose. It wanted to make sure neither Congress nor the people had too much power when picking the president. This system makes sure everyone in the country is represented. State leaders get a chance to fight for what their people want. Even though some don't like it, it's still a pretty good way to choose the President.

The Electoral College forces people who want to be president to try to get everyone in the country to like them. Just having one part of the country, like the South, on your side isn't enough to win. So, candidates have to try to win over people who might not like them at first. This is a good thing because a President that everyone likes will do a better job. All the attention on swing states gets people excited and teaches them about the issues. This helps them make a good decision about who should be president.

Keeping the Electoral College around is smart because it usually picks the same person the popular vote does. Back in 2000, Al Gore won the popular vote, but George W. Bush won because of the Electoral College. But stuff like that doesn't happen very often. The last time it happened before that was way back in 1888! The people who vote in the Electoral College are chosen by the parties, and they almost always vote for their party's candidate, like they're supposed to. Getting rid of the Electoral College and just using the popular vote could cause new problems. What if there's a tie? That would be awful! It's super rare, but it could happen. The Electoral College also helps when no one gets a majority of the votes.

Some people argue that the Electoral College makes people not want to vote in states where their candidate doesn't have a chance, like Republicans in California. But every vote still matters because it affects how the state's electoral votes are divided. People vote in presidential elections to show who they like, not just to be the one vote that decides everything. The Electoral College has worked well for over 100 years, and it will probably keep working well.

Want to save Earth? Easy: drive less! Check it out: a study in Europe says cars make about 12% of greenhouse gases. Giving up your car might look tough. But hold on, lots of options exist! Try carpooling, grab a ride, hop on a bus or train, use a taxi, or bike! It's not just about choices; it's about wanting to save Earth.

Cars bad for Earth! Cities are hitting back with fees to lower car use. Paris tried this out. After bad smog, they banned cars for a bit! This proves the world can change! Beijing, once super polluted like Paris, was cleaned up by the government. Bogota stops over 7.2 million from driving on "Day Without Cars." Mess up, and you pay a $25 fine or go to jail. Andrew Selsky said this "program looks set to spread to other countries, millions of Colombians hiked, biked, skated, or took buses to work during this car free day". It's not just cleaner air, but people hanging out in parks.

Fewer cars really help you! Less traffic makes air cleaner. More walking lowers heart problems, obesity, and such! Car companies might not like this. Ford, Toyota, Honda, etc., might lose cash if car sales fall, but some folks will still drive. But, less car making might help the economy by cutting gas costs. Guess what? Driving drops each year! Experts see this as good for Earth, even if car companies worry.

See? Car use drops! Young adults skip cars. A 2013 study saw a 23% drop in young people's driving from 2001-2009. Experts are cool, but the sales dip makes us wonder if the U.S. is at "peak driving." What now? Good or bad? Who knows! Obama’s goals to lower greenhouse gases seem to work. Studies now say "Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by".

Senator, isn't the Electoral College something? Should we stick with it, or go with whoever gets the most votes overall? I'm thinking popular vote. You know, a ton of people just don't bother voting, maybe around half. They figure their vote doesn't count, especially in states like California or Texas where it seems like the winner's already decided, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. Plus, candidates only seem to care about "swing states," totally ignoring everyone else.

Oh, and Senator, get this: "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. People just don't see the point if their candidate has no shot in their state. And some folks even tried to mess with the Electoral College back in the day. Segregationists tried to screw over Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy, it's in "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11, showing how wobbly it can be.

Now, I know there are reasons to keep the Electoral College. Some say it was a compromise by the Founding Fathers to be fair, giving "qualified citizens in congress" a voice, says "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Maybe it's good because people can influence electors, and history matters, "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15 says. The idea is electors vote for the winner, "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 notes. But even if electoral votes are right, the popular vote can still be higher—"In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 points out.

Senator, this winner-take-all thing isn't fair at all. Candidates spend all their time and cash in big states, blasting ads, and totally ignore smaller states they think they've already won. All the attention goes to "swing states." "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," it says in "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13. They're all about big and "swing states," which makes voters in ignored states feel bummed.

So, Senator, let's switch to a popular vote already! It would make people actually want to vote, knowing their vote matters and helps decide who's in charge. "Power to the People," that's what's important here.

Gosh, these days, it's like cars have taken over! It seems like nobody bothers walking or hopping on a bike anymore; folks just automatically drive. This really shakes things up, doesn't it? Is this how we want things to be? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky, they get this. They're all about cutting down on cars, but they've got their own reasons. After checking out their stuff, it hits you: fewer cars mean more free time, businesses that are doing better, resources that are saved, and this "car culture" thing, it's not as strong.

Are you constantly showing up late because you're stuck in traffic? Maybe walking or biking would actually get you there faster! Selsky was talking about this car-free day they had in Colombia. Millions of people were "hiking, biking, skating or took buses," so the roads were "eerily... devoid of traffic jams." Less traffic means less sitting around, twiddling your thumbs! Walking is not just good for the planet; it saves you time, too. Businesses can totally thrive when there are fewer cars around. Selsky mentions how "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" pop up, and those old sidewalks turn into "broad, smooth sidewalks." When people stroll around more, they spot more shops and buy more stuff!

Conserving resources is another huge perk of ditching the car more often. Rosenthal points out that cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-heavy areas." That's a big problem! We *have* to drive less because all those car fumes are seriously messing with the planet. Some cities even try out "no-driving" days. Duffer mentioned how Paris had a "partial driving ban" after they had "days of near-record pollution." Less driving equals less pollution, less traffic, and less of that nasty smog! That's awesome.

This whole car culture thing might be on its way out. Studies are showing that young Americans aren't driving as much these days. Rosenthal brings up a "23 percent" dip in driving among young folks. Maybe it's those pesky tickets, their parents nagging them, or just caring about the planet. But Rosenthal also said that experts think this is going to help big time with cutting down "carbon emissions and the environment." Because of this, car companies are switching things up, and tech is making commute times different.

Bottom line: less driving means more time in your day, businesses that are booming, resources that are being saved, and a car culture that's losing its grip. Next time you're heading somewhere, think about driving less. It's way better for our Earth.

So listen, the Electoral College? Gotta keep it. People whine it's old, but still the best for picking a President, no argument. Imagine: elections way easier than counting every single vote. No need to recount. Biggest thing? Stops big states from controlling everything. Keep the College? Stops total chaos.

Electoral College wins hardly ever cause the crazy fights you'd get if we just tallied all votes. Often, candidates get way more electoral votes than the popular vote says. Look at Obama. He got almost 62% of electoral votes, but only around 51% voted for him. With the "winner-take-all" thing in most states, figuring out each state's electoral votes is simple when someone gets the most, even if it’s not half. A tie? Could happen, sure, but super rare, even with an even number of total votes. Counting all votes? That’d be chaos.

The Electoral College stops us from needing runoff elections. Those happen when no one gets over half. Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92 got only about 43 percent of the popular vote, but still won the College with 301 and 370 votes. The College picks a clear winner, saving us the stress of another vote. Runoff votes are totally unnecessary.

So, really: the Electoral College stops big states from being too strong. For real, we gotta keep using it. Maybe better ideas exist, but the Electoral College is the best system we have working right now.

*Click!* My face lit up, and Mom took a photo with her iPhone. Holding this little piece of plastic feels like I can do anything. Ah, to be young and free! I was on my stomach in the kitchen, playing with a toy car while Mom cooked, the smell of garlic everywhere. Finally, I got my driver's license! It's huge for teens, but it's not all good. Less driving means less pollution, and we all breathe easier.

Now that I drive, I hate seeing smog that makes me squint on the way to school. My car adds to the problem! Car fumes are a big cause of smog, sending greenhouse gasses into the air. Cars in the U.S. cause about half of these emissions (Source 1). Even making gas causes pollution! It wastes energy and releases carbon. So, making fuel releases greenhouse gasses, and then our cars release even MORE! Thanks to AP Environmental Science, I know too many greenhouse gasses are bad. They trap sunlight and UV rays, causing global warming. Plus, smog is ugly. Paris was full of it, so they banned cars to clear it. After just one car-free day, the smog was gone! People could breathe again (Source 2). Smog hurts Paris' tourism. Who wants to see Versailles covered in pollution?

Using cars less is great for our bodies and minds, for sure. Take Vauban, Germany, where cars aren't allowed. Over half the people who moved there sold their cars and are happier! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was stressed. I'm much happier now." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that not driving is good for your mind. And your body feels great! People walk, run, bike, or skate to get around. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" every year, so people walk in the city (Source 3). Walking is amazing for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe fewer people would have heart problems.

So, driving less is good because it gives us cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you grab your keys to drive to your friend's, think about it. Think about your health, think about the planet, and maybe walk instead.

Elections matter! They don't happen all the time. Your vote matters. It helps decide the future of our country. It should be easy to pick the president. Just count the votes and find the winner. We should vote for who we really want. It's simple. The popular vote is much better than the confusing Electoral College.

Lots of folks think the popular vote is more fair. It's easy to understand: vote, count, and find the winner. Candidates try to visit different states and talk to people, if they can. But Bradford Plumer says the Electoral College makes candidates "ignore states they can't win, and focus on close races in 'swing' states." That doesn't seem right. Every state should get to hear from the candidates and learn about their ideas. Some candidates skip whole states. Plumer says that "during the 2000 election, seventeen states were ignored by candidates, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in 25 big media markets didn't see any campaign ads." How can voters know who to vote for if they don't see or hear from the candidates?

Also, the popular vote is easier to understand than the Electoral College, which can cause problems. The popular vote is simple: vote, count, and the most votes win. The Electoral College? Plumer says that if there's a tie, "the House of Representatives decides the election, with states voting for the president. The Senate picks the vice president. Each state gets one vote, so Wyoming's vote, for 500,000 people, is equal to California's 55 representatives, for 35 million voters." Can you imagine the mess? One vote would have so much power! The popular vote is clear: everyone votes, and the most votes win. We don't need representatives to mess things up.

In the end, the popular vote is better because it's fair. We pick who we want without anyone getting in the way. Plus, it's easier to understand than the Electoral College. A tie could make representatives vote, even if they don't know what people need. Voting is very important, so let's do it right. We should all vote for who we want, so others don't decide for us, including representatives. Voting matters!

Alright, so this whole Electoral College business? According to the Federal Register, here's how it goes: We cast our ballots for these electors. Afterwards, they get together and vote for both the President and Vice President. After that, Congress tallies up their votes. In essence, we're selecting individuals to ultimately choose the main person in charge. Seems alright, doesn't it? What’s all the complaining about?

A whole lot of people are pushing to throw it out in favor of a straight popular vote system. Bradford Plumer questions if these electors will truly vote in accordance with our preferences. He suspects they probably won’t! Well, maybe occasionally, but still we are choosing electors HOPING they'll choose correctly. We have to trust them. Even if we directly voted for the President, we would still have to trust THEY'D be up to the task. Essentially, it's fairly comparable, just with one additional step.

Richard A. Posner makes an interesting argument: "Why get rid of a system we already dislike?” He believes the Electoral College adds fairness, kind of like how the Senate benefits the smaller states. Exactly! The larger states have little Senate clout. So, if it were a straight popular vote, those big states would get even MORE attention since they hold all the population.

Plus, the Electoral College keeps things stable if nobody gets more than half the votes. Take '68, for example. Nixon got the most votes (43%), although not a majority. Clinton was similar in '92. Still, they both took the Electoral College, dodging the need for a runoff. That was close!

I'm in favor of keeping the Electoral College. It functions well, preventing states like Texas from dominating ones like Rhode Island. Also, it helps us avoid runoffs. Yes, some people say it’s outdated, but I still think the Electoral College is a pretty dependable way to vote.

Alright, listen up:

Electoral College? It's garbage – old, not fair, stupid! Folks try backing it, but they suck. Scared of voting straight up? Really? Even Dole knew: kill it! Reasons *for* it are just wrong. And get this: electors are untrustworthy! You *think* they'll vote right, but NOPE! No promise! Nixon, Carter, Dole, Chamber, even AFL-CIO – wanted it gone. People want votes to win, right? We pick *electors*, not the Prez. Tie? House picks, but votes AREN'T equal!

Electors? Not *really* voting for the president. Trust them to stick around? Nope! Voters pick *bad* electors? Yup! Electoral College? Broken, easy. People think Prez voting, but SURPRISE! It's a lie. We don't pick *electors*! Why think they're loyal? '60, Louisiana almost swapped electors to screw Kennedy. Nuts! Easy to cheat. Weak ground. "Faithless" electors betrayed folks. Might again. It's rigged! Kill it! Let people vote!

Electoral vote tied, House picks Prez and VP. That system? Just as bad! So unfair. Not democracy. Each state gets ONE vote. Wyoming's one rep, with, like, half a million folks, equals California's 55 reps, with 35 million! Makes sense? NO! That's the deal. People split their votes, one party for Prez, other for Congress. So House's pick could be wrong. Ties rare? Nope: 41,971 votes in '68 could've done it. 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii in '76 would have been it. Small changes, BIG problems. "Vote matters?" Crap. *This* system? Crazy it's here. People want one vote.

Seriously: ditch the electoral college. Direct voting. It's simple. Electors are weak. House is unequal. Let folks vote right! Let us vote! Prez "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Listen up, because constantly driving is really bad for our planet, so can't we all just drive less? The Earth is getting hammered and cities and the people in them are hurting. Cars suck everywhere, from here to Central America and even Europe - it's a global problem. Burning gas is frying the Earth, so how about we try to cut back on driving?

Paragraph 10 of the "Paris bans driving due to smog" article starts with "After days of near-record pollution..." So, what if Paris has horrible pollution? It really is awful! The "City of Love" is suffocating in smog! If Paris is issuing smog alerts, imagine what's happening in other places. We have more of everything - factories, cars - you name it. What can we do? It'll only get worse if we don't fix it, so let's wake up and do something now!

David Goldberg says something awesome in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph seven: "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why are cars more important than us? Sure, cars are fast, but what do we miss out on because of them? Forget gas for a second; what about our health? Our money goes to roads instead of parks, and we see malls instead of nature. Trees are awesome for clean air, but pollution is killing them off. Smog wasn't a thing before, but now cars are all anyone talks about!

Okay, so we need to drive less, but it's not hopeless. Maybe we could start with no-car days. Drive, and you get a ticket! They do it in Bogota, Colombia. There's even an article called "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," so maybe it'll work here! Cali and Valledupar are trying it out.

Okay, so, I went to California on the West Coast last summer. I was thinking it'd be cool, with surfing and mountains, all that stuff. I got to Los Angeles, thinking it would be a fun city, like Miami, but man, was I ever wrong! Seriously, the smog and bad traffic ruined how nice it looks. That happens in cities where people use cars a lot, but driving less would totally help. Cleaner air and safer streets? That'd be great for everyone!

Elizabeth Rosenthal said that "Cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution... and up to 50% in places with lots of cars" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). But trucks are making it worse too. Now everyone knows pollution is bad, so they want fewer cars. Places like France, Colombia, and the US are trying. And guess what? It's working! Rosenthal also said "Studies show that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting fewer licenses" (paragraph 29). Cars won't go away, but using them less helps the Earth.

Just think, if we had fewer cars in places like New York City, there'd be fewer accidents. There are about 500 car crashes every day in the US! And worldwide, it's over 5000! Car accidents kill many people, but we can stop them. Of course, drivers who think they're great and people on their phones make it worse. But check this out: cutting pollution also lowers car accidents. Duffer said, "Traffic went down 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Also, Bogota's Car Free Day is inspiring other countries in Latin America to lower traffic and make walking around safer.

Basically, people are starting to get that using fewer cars is really important. Less cars means cleaner air and less accidents, it's simple. Once I left the city and the highway on my trip, I finally got to see the pretty nature in California!

Alright, what exactly contaminates the air? It's mostly automobiles. However, driving less offers phenomenal upsides! Seriously, imagine this: fresher air for breathing, increased disposable income, and much safer roads for everyone. Should we get started?

Okay, when there are a lot of automobiles, the air just gets much dirtier. Remember Paris? During an acute air pollution event, they prohibited car use! And just like that, the air was less polluted! Reducing driving also cuts down on burning by those huge petroleum corporations. Conversely, you might instead ride your bike, stroll, get on the bus, skateboard, or take the train. Far better!

And guess what? Your bank account will appreciate it! Seriously, those exorbitant "traffic violation fines" folks get for driving illicitly? Gosh! Car ownership? Gasoline is exorbitant, in addition to maintenance and tickets. Driving less translates to more dough for you over time.

Finally, let’s address safety. One investigation demonstrated that modifying our mode of transportation – bicycling, walking, riding, buses – could raise safety. Reduced traffic results in less collisions, quite simply. Furthermore, fewer automobiles improves the safety for bikes, motorbikes, and people walking. Oh, and it diminishes the likelihood of driving under the influence too!

To put it simply, foregoing the automobile is excellent throughout: purer air, increased individual savings, and safer thoroughfares. With that said, what specifically motivates *you* to abstain from using your automobile every so often?

It seems a lot of folks think cars are a must these days. Even though everyone uses them to get around, cars mess up our planet big time and make walking or taking the bus seem lame. But hey, here's an idea: ditching the car more often is a super smart move! Other countries are doing it already, so we know it can work. If we had fewer cars, our planet, neighborhoods, and buses/trains could be way better.

Cars are really hurting our environment. Check out this article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" by Elisabeth Rosenthal. It talks about Vauban, Germany, and how they're trying to cut down on greenhouse gases. The article says that cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas. In the US, where everyone drives, it's even worse, like 50%! That's a lot of pollution, and it's making cities gross. If we didn't use cars so much, we could drop those numbers big time. Sometimes, governments step in to fight car pollution. For example, Robert Duffer wrote an article called "Paris bans driving due to smog." He says that Paris, France, stopped cars from driving because of really bad smog. "After days of crazy pollution, Paris only let certain cars drive to clean up the air. People with even-numbered license plates had to stay home or pay 22 euros. The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates' turn." And it worked! "Traffic dropped by 60% in Paris after five days of smog!" So, even small changes to how we drive can make a huge difference.

Cutting back on cars also makes cities way nicer. Andrew Selsky wrote an article called "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It's about how Bogota, Colombia, got better by using cars less. "The car-free day is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid-90s. They built tons of bike paths, the most in Latin America. Parks and sports spots popped up everywhere. They fixed the busted sidewalks, and now they're smooth. Rush-hour traffic is way better, and new restaurants and stores opened." Cities are now thinking about walkers, realizing that cars are a pain and trying to make things better for everyone. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal chats about the US trying to drive less. Bill Ford, who's in charge of Ford Motor Company, wants to work with phone companies to make cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, use less gas, make less pollution, and be safer." A lot of people are trying to make public spaces better for eco-friendly travel.

More and more, people get that we don't need cars as much, so they're driving less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal mentions fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America's love for cars is fading. The number of miles people drove peaked in 2005 and has been going down since, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also notes you don't need to own a car all the time. "The Internet lets you work from home and hang out online. Cities are getting cooler, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpool apps make it easier to get around, like shared vans for commuting. So, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not start again." Now, it's a breeze to snag a car when you need it, so you don't have to own one all the time.

Driving less is a good call. With fewer cars around, we can fix big problems like pollution. Cities are changing to make public spaces better for everyone. Relying less on cars is super important for making people's work, time, money, and lives better, both near and far.

Okay, as a young person with limited life experiences, I understand the Electoral College has been around for a while. Even though I'm not old enough to vote for a few more years, my thoughts as a 14-year-old could still be important. I really believe we should keep the Electoral College. If we picked a President just by who got the most votes overall, it could go very wrong. If that happened, one place might totally love the President, but most other places might hate them, which would be a huge problem.

If we chose our President by popular vote, we might end up with a really bad leader, seriously. In Article three, paragraph 20, it says that "The winner-take-all setup makes candidates focus on swing states." These states are super important for deciding who wins, so people there are more informed. But with a popular vote, anyone over 18 could vote based on silly reasons. Young people might pick their favorite candidate because of their friends or what party they like. Lots of people would make quick choices without really thinking; the Electoral College stops that from happening.

Also, a popular vote could elect a president just because they're famous in one part of the country. Article three, paragraph 19, points out that someone who's only popular in one region won't be a good president, because no single region has enough votes to elect someone on their own. Just imagine if the popular vote was everything. Let's say Rodney is super popular in the South. Because Rodney won't get more electoral votes, he doesn’t need to campaign there. If being popular was all that mattered, voters in other places might feel ignored, and the President wouldn't care about them. They'd still feel like he wasn't "their president," even if he won. The Electoral College prevents this. We vote for electors, and then they vote for the president. Winning the most votes means all the state’s electors go to them; this is fair. A popular vote unfairly hurts the people who didn't vote for the winner.

I know some people disagree with me. Article two says, "Candidates don’t spend time in states they can’t win... Seventeen states didn't even see the candidates in 2000!" Candidates don't bother with states they're likely to lose. I get the argument for the popular vote, which would make candidates visit every state. But there are reasons why I don't agree with that. Just because a candidate goes to all 50 states doesn't mean they'll win. The Electoral College makes sure a state’s majority matters, not just overall popularity. Think of it like high school: the popular kid might not be nice, but some of the less popular kids are. Picking a President based on popularity could be bad for our country.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College is really important. A popular vote could give us a bad president or one who only wins because one region really likes them. Please think about what I've said when you talk about this.

Consider a much better world, one where people simply drove less. It could be a planet safer and healthier!

Seriously, just think about having less cars on the road. The air all around us could turn out to be a lot cleaner! Wouldn't it be easier for everybody to breath in cleaner air?

While some people might be annoyed by the thought of not using their car, it really does help everyone in the end. What if you had to constantly breathe in dirty air? It is pretty bad for you, especially for your lungs! Also, driving less will mean that you won't have to visit the gas station as much. Saving money is really awesome, don't you think? That money could be used on practically anything!

Cars are definitely not cheap! Make sure to remember monthly car insurance and how you have to fill up gas weekly. Even if gas prices are low right now, they could suddenly rise, thus emptying your wallet.

So, is driving really worth what it costs to the earth and the money in your wallet?

Would life be much more convenient if our cars were not used as much?

Ultimately, the decision is yours!

Have you ever considered all the pollution your car creates? I bet not. You're most likely concentrating on where you need to go or on texting, and certainly not the environment.
