It's understandable since the environment isn't always a main concern! Usually we only pay attention when a crazy story about really bad air quality comes on the news.

Because car pollution accumulates very slowly, the harm it causes to the planet is easy to just ignore.

If this keeps going on, then both plants and people might end up in the hospital, barely able to breathe!

Envision not being stressed over car payments or always having to fill up on gas.

So many people and entire families are always stressed over money, as they are trying to pay their bills and afford gas just to get to work.

Instead of your car constantly using gas, what would happen if you tried to walk or bike to work? Picture all the money you'd save!

Perhaps dumping the Electoral College isn't such a stellar idea. The Constitution’s authors included it to balance Congressional power with regular citizens. Some gripe it's currently unjust, as electors technically pick the president, not us – we merely vote for them. But electors changing votes? Rare, usually just talk. So, despite claims of unfairness, we ought to keep the Electoral College. It streamlines matters, lessens extra ballots, and empowers smaller states.

Sure, the Electoral College gets tricky, like in 2000, but that’s rarer than battling over popular vote counts. Fact is, someone can win the Electoral College without winning the popular vote. Back in 2012, Obama snagged 61.7% of the electoral vote with only 51.3% of the popular vote. The winner-takes-all rule can blow up a small win into something huge.

And also, the Electoral College sidesteps elections where no one gets over half the votes. Look at 1968 and 1992, where winners only got around 43% of the vote. This could mean ties and more voting! But the Electoral College picks one winner outright. Some even suggest it stops people from voting in states where their choice is sure to lose, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. No point if it’s pointless, agreed?

Nevertheless, the Electoral College levels the playing field, offering small states a shot against the big boys. In 2012, Florida was razor-close, and its 29 votes went to Obama. Candidates try harder in big states because they matter more in the Electoral College. People complain about the winner-take-all thing, but candidates target swing states instead of wasting time where the outcome's clear.

Sure, some might skip voting thinking their vote barely matters, but one vote hardly ever decides an election. People vote to show what they believe, not because they think they're the sole decider. That’s why the Electoral College, not only the popular vote, should still pick our leader, plain and simple.

The electoral college? Seriously, it feels so wrong. Richard A. Posner tried to defend it in "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," but come on, Florida in 2012? Obama snagged all the electoral votes with just 62% of the vote! What about the 48% who didn't vote for him? Their votes meant squat! Winner takes all, even if it's a tight race. Bradford Plumer nails it in "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong"—swing states get all the love. Back in 2000, candidates basically ghosted seventeen states. Only those swing states really mattered. With a popular vote, everyone gets attention, right? Every single vote counts the same, wherever you live.

Plus, this electoral college thing could cause real trouble. Plumer points out electors sometimes go rogue and vote against the grain. It's rare, yeah, but it could happen! Just one person could throw everything into chaos. And a tie? Each state gets one vote? How messed up is that? Millions in big states? Their vote is worth the same as some tiny state? A popular vote solves all of this mess.

Richard A. Posner tries to make a case for the electoral college, but his reasons are weak. He thinks a popular vote would lead to more squabbles? Where's the evidence? He claims candidates would only hit up their own backyards? Maybe, but they'd lose, wouldn't they? He says the electoral college educates swing state voters? Really? A popular vote would educate *everyone*. He thinks it helps big states? Nope. Candidates will chase the crowds, plain and simple. Runoff elections? Okay, that's a valid point. They could be a headache. But it's still better than being unfair or inviting disaster!

So, it's tough to defend this electoral college thing. There's plenty of proof that it's unfair and risky. A national popular vote? We need it, badly. When you're backing the electoral college? You're backing unfairness, and maybe even a complete mess. Nobody wants that!

America loves life, freedom, and being happy! We also believe in fairness and a good shot for everyone. But, the Electoral College? It's strange. People called electors from each state vote for president. Is that fair? People argue. Lots think it's bad, and there are reasons.

Imagine: you vote for someone you like, and someone becomes an elector for that person, but then, wow, that elector changes their vote! You get someone you don't even like. That feels wrong. You can't fully trust the electors. Wouldn't it be easier to count all the votes? Whoever gets the most votes wins, not some person that nobody wanted. It's happened: the one with the most votes lost because of the Electoral College, and your favorite didn't get to be president. Voting fair is important. We fought for it.

Besides votes that seem unsure, the Electoral College makes more trouble. A tie seems rare, but history has close races. What if it's tied? Some say the Electoral College makes it easier, but maybe it doesn't. A tie is easier with fewer electors. One elector changing their mind is like thousands of votes changing. Also, electors get prizes when their pick wins, even if their state or the country helped and should get some credit.

So, the Electoral College makes us wonder about fairness and who to trust. People don't get to fully control electors, and ties happen more. America believes in being equal, so direct, fair votes are what we need.

Have you ever just considered the impact our obsession with cars has? Just picture it: yucky air, smoggy skies, and resources going away. Think about having to wear a mask just to breathe because of smog. But, if we try "green" options and drive less, we can make our towns better and help the Earth. We could have cleaner air, better places to live, and cooler ways to get around. Seriously, go green and be ready to be wowed!

Consider the nasty smog caused by all of us driving, most notably in big cities! The emissions from cars trapped in the air hurt the air and make it hard to breathe. Fun fact, Paris once limited car use due to bad smog. They banned cars on some days, which really helped with the smog! At one point, Paris's smog was close to Beijing, China's smog. But, after the smog went away, cars came back, and it was bad again.

Good towns actually have fewer cars. Take Vauban, a town in Germany with no cars allowed. About 70% of people there don't have cars, and around 57% sold their cars to move there! Heidrum Walter, who lives in Vauban, said, "Having a car was always stressful. Now, I feel way better." Bogota, in Colombia, wants to cut down on cars too. These lifestyles without cars are good for our health and the Earth!

Okay, cars aren't going anywhere, but there are other ways to get around now. In NYC, bike rentals are getting popular. Carpooling is also becoming a thing to help reduce cars on the streets. New data shows that less young people in the US are getting licenses and buying cars. Think about a future with fewer cars!

So, depending less on cars is super helpful for the Earth. It can decrease smog, and the Earth will become more green!

Goldberg, from Transportation for America, which backs a life without cars, notes that after WWII, infrastructure favored cars. He argues we need a fresh perspective. The world’s changing, and cars, formerly crucial, are becoming less vital. Let’s explore why car ownership is decreasing.

This change is speeding up, as shown in Germany. Vauban, started in 2006, is a leader in "smart planning," designing a car-free place. More people in suburbs now walk, bike, or use trams for work or school. "Smart planning" shapes towns to cut down on driving. In Vauban, a whopping 70% of families don't own cars and are more content, no longer stressed by daily driving. A stunning 57% even sold their cars to live in Vauban! Car-free towns are popping up worldwide, making cities and suburbs more packed, reducing how far people need to travel.

A big reason is the need to fight global warming. Cars are big greenhouse gas emitters, making up about 12% of the total worldwide, and even up to 50% in car-heavy places like the US.

Paris, struggling with bad smog, stopped cars with even-numbered plates from driving, fining those who broke the rule, which greatly reduced traffic. The smog was due to using diesel, which cost less than gas. Because of this success, Paris also banned cars with odd-numbered plates, given its role as the capital and its ongoing smog issues.

Cars have hugely shaped our lives. Yet, studies in the US show fewer cars bought, less driving, and fewer people getting licenses. Despite America’s car history, more people want a greener world, seeing the perks of life without cars. The troubles of insurance, payments, repairs, safety worries, and overall stress of owning a car all play a role. While cars are handy, their downsides are becoming clear. Fewer teens and young adults are getting licenses, and more skip car ownership completely. This begs the question: why even own a car?

Across the globe, people are thinking about the good and bad of cars, realizing the benefits of less driving, and changing how they live. One of history’s biggest inventions is starting to be less important.

The planet's changing quickly! Folks are really keen on lowering greenhouse gasses. Driving less or even getting rid of cars is a big way to do it. Crazy, huh? Yet some places nail it, and it’s working! They’re really cutting down on car pollution.

Take Vauban, Germany, near Freiburg. No parking, driveways, or garages! Instead, folks jump on a tram zooming into Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, a media teacher with two kids, digs it. Vauban, done in 2006, shows a rising trend in Europe and the US: living in the suburbs but not needing cars all the time. This one-square-mile place lets its 5,500 residents easily walk to shops and stuff on the main street.

Europe makes about 12% of the world’s greenhouse gasses, but the US cranks out a huge 50%! Think Paris – the smog got so awful, pollution went wild. The city reacted by stopping certain cars. One day, cars with even-number plates were off-limits. Next, it was odd numbers. If you broke the rule, you got a fine, but the city made public transport free all week.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars" to get people to drive less. Millions of Colombians found other ways to get to work. This city of seven million urged folks to try different ways to get around to fight the smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus said that even rain didn’t stop people. That year, Cali and Valledupar, two more Colombian cities, joined in. Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, said, "These folks are starting something big, and it’s catching on!" Besides the "Day Without Cars," Bogota has rush-hour rules, better sidewalks, and new places to eat and shop.

Vauban's car-free setup, Paris's temporary car ban, and Bogota's car-free day show how we can drive less. People want change to cut greenhouse gasses. Car registrations in the US are down, and fewer young people are getting licenses – a 23% drop from 2001 to 2009! The aim? Save resources, cut greenhouse gasses, and be safer. With cities joining in, it looks like we can do it!

Want to drive less? Great news all around! First off, spending less time driving equates to fewer nasty pollutants released into the air. Shockingly, cars in some places could be responsible for a crazy 12% to even half of all pollution! Seriously, cutting back on driving is like giving the Earth a big, cleansing breath, making for a much healthier planet for everyone!

Here's something to consider: Maybe getting rid of that daily drive could mean saying “bye-bye” to stress! Making your way to work or school is often mentally draining, and the mere idea of getting stuck in traffic can cause you to feel anxious. What’s interesting is that some places have seen traffic get way better simply by making small changes during those crazy rush hour times. So, choosing other ways to get around or just making those car trips shorter can bring some much-needed peace to our super-busy lives.

It seems the younger generation is figuring out that driving less is actually pretty good. Numbers show that fewer young people are even bothering to get their driver's licenses. A study recently showed that almost one-fourth fewer young people are driving these days. What does this mean? Well, possibly fewer crashes and a safer world for everyone, especially the youngsters.

Basically, a bunch of good things happen when we decide to drive less. From lowering pollution and stress to making things safer for younger people, thinking about taking other routes instead of always driving makes way for nicer, more lasting communities.

Word on the street is something's changing! Politicians and regular people want to drive less now. Check this out: a town in Germany is almost totally car-free. Guess what? The people living there seem really happy! Germany isn't the only place that wants cleaner air and less stress from cars. Even Paris, France is trying out this "no-car" thing. Sometimes, Paris stops cars when the air is super dirty. Then, after a week, the air is way cleaner! Vauban in Germany and Paris are both trying different ways to cut back on cars. But hold on, Colombia has *three* cities trying the same thing! They have a "no car" day once a year.

So, why did Vauban in Germany build a city with so few cars and parking spaces? Most suburbs have houses far apart, hardly any stores or fun things nearby, and tons of cars. It feels like all suburbs are the same! But all those cars cause pollution. Vauban fixed this by making it harder to use cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." Cars make up about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, the article says. But in some places in the U.S., it's almost 50%! Vauban helps clean the air just by using fewer cars. They built walkways and stores instead of just roads, which makes it easier to get around. Money saved on roads goes to other types of transportation. The article says that usually, 80% of transportation money goes to highways. Only 20% goes to other things.

Paris is really working hard to reduce how much people drive. The city has so much to see, and you can get almost anywhere on a bike. No one wants to make the city more polluted by driving! That's why Paris sometimes bans cars. The city's pollution can be as bad as Beijing, China (which is known for being polluted). Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." Paris first wanted to ban cars completely to stop pollution. But they knew that wouldn't work with so many people driving. So, they made a deal. On some days, cars with even license plates couldn't drive. The next day, odd plates were banned. This went on for a week. If you broke the rules, you had to pay 22 euros. Duffer's article said almost 4,000 drivers paid that week! Even with the fines, traffic went down 60%!

Bogota, Colombia is another city trying to limit cars. Almost seven million people live in Bogota. They have a car-free day every year. People walk, bike, skate, and take the bus. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The city is trying to cut down on pollution, traffic, and accidents by pushing taxis, buses, and other ways to get around, the article says. After three years of success, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) joined in. The car-free day was part of a plan to fix up Bogota that started in the mid-1990s, according to Selsky. Because of this plan, Bogota built 118 miles of bike paths! Plus, they built parks and sports centers.

Even in the U.S. (where people drive a *lot*), things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "The End Of Car Culture." The article says that for the past 8 years, fewer young people have been getting licenses and driving. More people are walking, biking, taking the bus, or using taxis. Traffic is getting worse, so these options are often faster than driving. When people do drive, they share rides more. This helps lower air pollution.

It appears many folks are leaning towards ditching the Electoral College. Back in 2000, a Gallup survey revealed a preference for a straightforward popular vote setup. However, us Americans need to ponder every avenue. Perhaps the Electoral College had its purpose previously, but now seems like the moment for a switch-up.

Choosing electors morphs into a muddled and lengthy ordeal within the Electoral College. Initially, states must handpick their electors, succeeded by the planning and execution of meetings. Come election day, deciding who qualifies as an elector and the reasoning behind it can become a true head-scratcher. These electors subsequently cast their ballots for the President and Vice President; after this, votes get tallied up. Boasting 538 members, the Electoral College demands a minimum of 270 votes to snag the presidential win. A direct election could genuinely streamline this whole shebang.

Under a direct election format, each individual vote carries weight, potentially fostering greater unity among the states. People crave assurance that they possess worth. As the Electoral College's popularity wanes, numerous individuals are warming up to the idea of a direct vote as a superior means of choosing the President and Vice President.

Each contender selects a cohort of electors within each state; frequently these individuals get the nod from their political party. Should someone disapprove of the chosen electors, they might feel their vote carries no significance and their voice is unheard in shaping the presidency.

In essence, the Electoral College is just not vibing with people anymore. Its clunkiness and time-consuming nature irk them. Certain individuals suggest officials are hanging onto it to guard their power. A direct election promises to unscramble the mess and simplify the selection of our leader.

From the dawn of time, humans have sought ways to simplify their lives, inventing tools for hunting and constructing carts. Back then, horse poop posed a MAJOR challenge! Everyone had to constantly clean it up. Then, suddenly! Cars appeared. Sounds great, doesn't it? But no, they created smog.

Recently, Paris banned certain vehicles due to severe smog levels. This change disrupted Paris's climate. Nights became colder, and days became warmer. Some blame dirty diesel fuel, but honestly, anything that burns fuel contributes to smog, obviously! However, take a look at Vauban in Germany. It’s entirely car-free! People there don't own cars because public transportation is excellent. Vauban residents say they feel significantly less stressed compared to when they had to deal with cars. Kids play safely without cars, potentially reducing their screen time. Does Vauban seem extreme? Well, Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day annually. Only buses and taxis are allowed, and violators face steep fines. Even in the U.S., young adults are increasingly less interested in driving. From 2003 to 2009, driving decreased by 23%. Driving is losing popularity because there are numerous other engaging activities. Is this the beginning of a change? In April 2013, people drove as little as in 1995, when fewer people owned cars. New York City even has a carpool program, believe it or not?!

Sadly, some areas, like Florida, have poor public transportation. Want to travel from Homestead to Miami? Ugh! You might drive to a bus stop, take a bus to a train station, ride a train for an hour, then take another bus for thirty minutes. That totals three hours EACH way. A staggering thirty hours per week! Driving only takes an hour, making it the better option. Simply running errands in downtown Miami or Doral from Homestead can take up your entire day by car. Without a car, you could waste DAYS, or need to wake up incredibly early.

Looking at Vauban, people seem happier than those who rely on cars. They socialize more because they walk frequently. The air is fresh, unlike in smog-filled cities. Cars account for about 12% of Europe's pollution and over 50% in the States! If Paris had acted earlier, perhaps it wouldn't have so much smog. After WWII, we invested heavily in cars. Imagine how much better medicine or other fields could be with that amount of money. In the U.S., highways receive more funding than public transportation. Vauban and Bogota succeed because shops and homes are close, and buses operate smoothly. Completely eliminating cars may be challenging given our reliance on them, but we can gradually change.

Hey, check it out! I'm super worried about the really bad air pollution all over our country. I had a thought: what if we didn't use cars so much? Seriously, if we did this one simple thing, it could make a huge difference in the pollution problem. Cars are a big reason for it. Plus, it could help us feel closer to each other and make our towns way better.

Like I said, cars cause a lot of air pollution. So, if we drove less, there would be fewer nasty pollutants going into the air. Less exhaust means less carbon messing up our climate. And you know what? That means it's easier to breathe and everyone's healthier. We've already seen good things happen in places where people don't drive as much. Also, get this: "Car-Free Day" is getting popular in places like Columbia and other countries in Central America. Less exhaust also means less of that gross smog that makes the air dirty. If we cut back on car fumes, we can stop problems like the really bad pollution in cities like Paris.

But wait, there's more! If we drive less, we can actually hang out with each other more. Imagine walking around, chatting with your neighbors, or biking to the park. People who live in places where there aren't many cars, like Vauban, say they feel less stressed without all the cars around. And here's a cool fact: young people don't even care that much about cars! They'd rather take the bus or share a ride, so they can spend more time with their friends and family.

Think about this: because we're always driving, sidewalks and bike paths don't get much attention. But if we didn't drive as much, those old sidewalks and bumpy roads would get fixed right away. In Columbia, "Car-Free Day" led to new sidewalks and tons of bike lanes – over a hundred miles! They also made more parks, sports fields, and cool places to shop. So, driving less can help us clean up our towns, make more parks, and build more places to have fun. Plus, it can make things safer by lowering the chances of car accidents and making streets safer in general.

So, everyone, I really want you to think about driving less. It can really help us. We can have cleaner air, be healthier, and feel more connected to each other.

Taking it easy on cars – maybe even changing how we move around – is a great idea. Less traffic? Awesome! Cities choked by smog, like L.A. and Beijing, could actually breathe again. Drivers would feel less stressed and save money. Who doesn’t love saving money?

Smog is a major issue in busy cities. You can practically taste it in L.A., and Beijing often has a smog cloud over it. Cars make a ton of pollution. In Europe, cars cause 15% of the air pollution, and in the U.S., that number skyrockets to 50%! Fewer cars equals less pollution. If you can’t get rid of your car, think about a hybrid like a Prius. Some cities, like Bogota, do “Car-Free Days.” They’re a hit, so other cities are trying them too. These days really help clear the air. Paris even blocked cars when the smog got really bad, but hybrids and carpools were okay. That shows how serious smog can be.

Driving can be a pain. Traffic jams mean you’re always late and grumpy. Drivers get mad when someone cuts them off and worry about crashes. Imagine no more of that stress! In Vauban, Germany, they’ve almost gotten rid of cars to make life simpler. People have cars, but they keep them in a pricey garage outside town (about $40,000!). You can walk everywhere – shops, restaurants, the works. Cars are mainly for long trips. Walking to get your mail or enjoying nature without cars cuts down on stress. Plus, you don’t have to dodge traffic to grab a burger! With phones and the internet, staying in touch without driving is easy. Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram keep you connected, so you don't have to drive as much.

Finally, fewer cars mean more money in your pocket. In places like Vauban, where there aren’t many cars, you might not need one at all. A decent car can set you back $30-40k. Think of what you could do with that cash! A sweet house, an amazing vacation, or even pay for your kid’s school. And everything is close enough to walk to. Sounds pretty good, right?

A regular popular vote, unlike the electoral college, which also aims to pick an election winner, could cause a lot of issues. The electoral college makes things easier and chooses a president in a simpler way, stopping problems before they start. It may not be perfect, but it's way better than the alternative.

To be fair, a popular vote does have some good points. Richard A. Posner says in "In Defense of the Electoral College" that the electoral college uses electors, not regular voters, to make the final choice ("source 3 a15"). But think about this: a popular vote gives power straight to the people, without any go-betweens. But honestly, do voters even know who these electors are? Bradford Plumer rightly points out in "The Indefensible Electoral College" that states choose the electors, leaving voters out ("source 2 a 10"). That's not very democratic.

Even if voters feel unimportant, electors usually vote as their state wants. Also, the electoral college makes it super easy to announce a winner. No ties! Posner says that "The winning candidate always gets a bigger share of the electoral vote than the popular vote," meaning that even a close popular vote win turns into a big electoral vote win for that state ("source 3 a 15"). Makes sense?

The electoral college also makes candidates talk to different groups of people across the country. Posner says, "No single area has enough votes to win an election, so the president has to appeal to more people" ("source 3 a 19"). Basically, to win, a candidate needs to win over people from all over. And swing states are super important, with smart voters who decide everything. Posner thinks that "People in swing states decide the election and are the smartest voters" ("source 3 a 20"). Candidates better listen up!

While some people might want a popular vote, the electoral college makes presidential elections more organized and better. It makes sure the process is standard and encourages candidates to talk to regular people by campaigning in different places.

Senator Rick Scott, the Electoral College is definitely something folks are talking about a lot when elections roll around. It's all about those 538 votes, with a candidate needing a solid 270 to snag the win. The more folks you have in a state, the bigger its voice. Take California, booming with 55 votes, while little Rhode Island only gets 3. Remember back when Al Gore lost in 2000, despite winning the popular vote? That really fired up the debate about the Electoral College. While some folks are all about ditching it, I'm not on board. It's a cornerstone of our presidential elections. It ensures someone wins fair and square, picks a president for "Everyone", values those sharp voters in swing states, and nips those runoff elections in the bud.

Without it, choosing a winner would be a real headache. The system is set up to always get us a winner. And if it's somehow tied, the Senate steps in. Each senator gets a vote, so even the smaller states get a chance to speak up. Plus, candidates have to win over different parts of the country. The president is supposed to represent the whole nation, not just one area. Now, swing states become kingmakers. States like Florida, New York, Texas, and California that can really flip an election. Winning these states is a massive leg up. These states matter, they have tons of people, but also many voters who are on the ball. In swing states, voters really pay attention and keep a close eye on the candidates. The Electoral College also stops the chaos of no one getting a clear majority. Like when Bill Clinton won with just 43% of the popular vote but still grabbed 370 electoral votes. This cuts the need for another vote. So, even though some folks say, we should ditch the Electoral College since we vote for electors, it makes sure someone wins clearly, which is key.

So, let's stick with the Electoral College. With its 538 votes, where you need 270 to win, and those tie-breaking rules, it makes sure we get a clear winner and elects a president for "Everyone", acknowledges educated voters in swing states, and stops runoff elections.

Without a doubt, cars have a huge impact on the entire world. Whether you know it or not, using cars less would really benefit everyone and the Earth too. Smart city designs that focus on different options can lead to big improvements, like cleaner air, safer and quieter streets, and a much more relaxed feeling everywhere.

"Cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases… but in US areas where cars are super common, that number can jump to 50%." Basically, cars pollute a lot, mainly in cities packed with them. Someone said "...future suburbs could be like Vauban, not just in rich countries, but also in poorer ones where more middle-class folks own cars and worsen the pollution." Most experts agree: fewer cars means less air pollution. Take Paris, "After days of bad smog, Paris restricted driving to clean the air." Fewer cars in cities means less smog, which means better air for everyone and the environment.

Unfortunately, car accidents happen all the time, and they're often deadly. While not every accident can be stopped, many can be. Just think of all the lives lost in busy cities or because of stressed and mad drivers. One driver confessed, "I used to be so tense while driving. Now I'm way more chill." For many, driving is a pain. Using cars less often could mean less stress for everyone. Picture a nicer place with kids playing and bikes instead of loud horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads take up a lot of room. If more people walk, bike, and drive less, we won't need so many roads, and we can have bigger sidewalks. Cities are really trying to get people to use cars less, "...trying to make suburbs smaller..." One way to do this is to put stores close together on main streets so people can walk instead of driving far to malls. This helps everyone stay safer.

In conclusion, it's better to drive less. It lowers pollution and accidents, and it makes our living areas better and quieter. The world would be much nicer, and daily life would get a lot better, if cities and countries would work on lowering how much we drive.

Do we use cars way too much now?

Remember when Paris banned cars because of smog, like Source 2 said? The air was so bad that they only let some cars drive! The smog stuck around for days, almost beating records! Using cars so much makes smog and hurts the earth. But it's okay, we can change! Driving less has lots of good things about it.

What if we didn't need cars? VAUBAN, a town in Germany, did it! They're seeing if no cars makes people happier and healthier. Going to and from work can be annoying with traffic, noise, and crashes. Sources 1 and 3 say "70% of families don't have cars, and 57% got rid of theirs when they moved there. 'I was always stressed with a car. I'm happier now,' said Heidrun Walter." This shows not having cars can make you feel better.

Cleaner air is another big plus when you drive less. When the air got really bad, Paris stopped some cars from driving to fight the smog. Almost 4,000 drivers got tickets, and some had their cars taken away for a bit because they protested the fines. But guess what? It helped! Car traffic in Paris went down by 60% in just days. The air got better, so the French government let more cars drive on some days.

Are Americans tired of cars? Or maybe new stuff is making us not need to drive as much? Elisabeth Rosenthal says "the internet lets you work from home and talk online. Cities are getting better, so suburbs aren't as nice. Also, smartphones and ride-sharing apps are easy options, like vans for work" (source 4, 35). Because of tech, we don't need to meet in person as much since we can use apps, calls, texts, and video calls.

After thinking about all of this, I think driving less helps everyone. I hope you think so too!

Cars! Everywhere, you look they're there. Red ones, blue ones, even blah black ones, with constant beeps and honks. Sure, they get us places. But what if they vanished? Could ditching them actually be a good thing? Turns out, saying "bye-bye" to cars just might save the planet and chill us out.

First off, fewer cars? Huge win for the Earth! Cars are gas-guzzling polluters, spewing out greenhouse gasses. In Europe, they're responsible for 12% of these gasses. But in some US spots, where everyone drives, it's a whopping 50%! Paris once even banned cars for a bit to fight smog. After only five days, traffic was down a crazy 60%! Less cars equals less yuck in the air, which makes Earth happy. Also, young adults drove much less from 2001 to 2009 -- like, 23% less! Experts think this trend will keep going, maybe cutting carbon emissions 'cause, let's be real, transport pollutes the US a whole lot.

But hold on, there's another perk! Kicking cars to the curb can boost our bodies and minds. Think about Vauban, a car-free German town. One resident used to stress about cars but is now way happier. Less traffic, less road rage? Obvi less stress. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars," and folks dig it. Someone said it’s super chill. Less stress equals better mental health. And get this: In Bogota, that car-free day pushes people to walk, bike, skate, or hop on a bus to work. Exercise makes you happy! Your brain pumps out feel-good stuff. So, fewer cars could mean more moving around, which means happier, healthier people.

Okay, so ditching cars could mean a happier planet and happier peeps. Yeah, cars are handy, but they've got problems. Next time you reach for your keys, maybe think twice about driving. Think about the clean air cars steal. And remember the anger and stress cars cause.

Ever wonder why roads jam up, especially around 5 PM? The reason is clear: we drive way too much. Reducing our driving offers lots of advantages, genuinely making life better.

Sure, cars might seem like a fast way to get around at first. However, this quickness doesn't make up for all the downsides. One big problem? Constant driving makes us lazy. Walking or running is great exercise, but we miss out when we always drive. My friend Walter mentioned that owning a car used to stress him out, but he felt much better without it. We ought to cut back on driving and discover fun, easy ways to move around. Walking might take longer, but it pays off. No more traffic headaches, and you can see spots cars can't reach. Plus, you can explore new paths, not just roads. Rain might stop you from walking, but Antanas Mockus, a former Bogota mayor, believed rain shouldn't keep us inside. Cars keep us dry, sure, but walking is much safer. Cities like Germany, Paris, and Bogota are trying to go "car-free" because of these issues.

Also, less driving could mean less road rage. People often get mad at each other when driving, usually from stress. If we drive less, we'll be calmer. Car crashes are serious, and many die because of them. Driving less could save lives. Cars cause more problems than they solve. Walking and biking are easier and safer. This would also help traffic flow, making it easier for everyone to move around. Drunk driving is a huge problem that takes lives. A drunk person walking isn't as risky as a drunk person driving. Think about the downsides of too much driving.

Finally, Barack Obama said we should drive less to fight pollution. Reports say Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting licenses less often, which is great. Paris banned cars because of terrible smog, stopping them from entering certain areas. Diesel fuel made the smog worse. In France, 67% of cars use diesel, while only 53% do in other Western European countries. Bogota wants to "make other ways of getting around more appealing and lower smog." Violators get fined. They learned that driving less can lower stress and clean up the air. Bill Ford imagines cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, use less gas, lower pollution, and stay safer." These are only a few benefits of cutting back on driving. Going car-free or driving less would be a big change. Let's end pollution, prevent crashes, and stop underage driving. Consider all the bad things about driving too much compared to driving less.

Okay, so we've been battling pollution and warming. Can't we just solve this already? Cars are a HUGE issue. People just need to drive less frequently! Some places are actually stepping up. Take Vauban, Germany: absolutely no cars! And in Paris, France, only specific license plates can drive on different days. Meanwhile, Bogota, Colombia is also taking action!

Consider Vauban, Germany. There are no driveways or parking spaces! So, people have to shell out big bucks for parking garages. Some say cars are about 12% of Europe's pollution. It sounds small, but it isn't! If we make these car-free zones, there will be less pollution, as people won't drive as much. Heidrun Walter mentioned, "I was always stressed with a car. I am happier now!" That proves life is easier sans car! Less spent on gas and repairs equals less money stress, making life simpler. So, costly parking in cities is good for the planet and people's lives.

What about doing what Paris, France, does? Odd plates on Monday, even plates on Tuesday. The pollution was terrible, even worse than Beijing! But guess what? Pollution went down by 60% in only FIVE DAYS after they started. Insane, right? This shows these car rules are effective and quick!

Then you have "no car days," like in Bogota, Colombia. Carlos Arturo Plaza said it perfectly: "It's a good way to reduce stress and air pollution," showing how cars and bad stuff are linked. The source also says Cali and Valledpar, other cities in Colombia, are also doing it. Plus, leaders from other countries are paying attention and are impressed. That means this idea could spread! Definitely, this is a good way to get people to drive less.

So, yeah, cars are a problem. But many people drive without thinking about the pollution they make. Good ideas are: cities with paid parking, odd-even plates, and "no car days."

The U.S. belongs to us, the citizens. We're in control, and it's all for our benefit! We cast votes for our leaders, a process viewed by many as the most effective method for selecting the President and Vice President, correct?

However, the Electoral College presents a contentious issue. While electors might possess considerable knowledge about the nation and believe they have superior judgment, their experiences often differ significantly from ours. Ordinary citizens understand the challenges they confront daily. As Bradford Plummer aptly stated, "the best reasons for it don't make sense in the real world" (source 14). They may grasp the intricacies of Washington, but they frequently overlook the struggles of everyday people.

Public involvement in government is vital. If the Electoral College alone chose the President, people would feel excluded and unheard. Maintaining stability requires popular consent. Without it, unrest could easily arise. Richard A. Posner argues that people would "feel like their vote doesn't matter, thinking the new president doesn't care about them and isn't really their leader" (source 19). Consider this: people already get upset when their candidate loses NOW, imagine their anger if they had no say at all!

The Electoral College is simply unjust. Richard A. Posner highlighted that some support it due to "its not-democratic beginnings" (source 17). The popular vote functions effectively. Why complicate matters unnecessarily? While change can be positive, it isn't always so.

In essence, eliminating the popular vote would be a grave error, fundamentally unfair, and detrimental to the nation. It contradicts the core principle of "we the people."

Despite its issues, the Electoral College has those who defend it. Each state's electoral votes equal its representation in Congress (House + Senate = Electors!). There are 538 electors total. You need a majority, 270 or more, to win. People who like it say it can make a clear winner, ensures many people are represented, affects swing states, gives more power to bigger states, and prevents recounts.

Basically, the Electoral College tries to make sure presidential elections are fair and safe from being messed with. Even though it's the final say, it has problems. The "winner-takes-all" way, where whoever wins the most votes in a state gets all the votes, seems unfair. Candidates might focus on states with lots of people because they have more electoral votes. Someone who wins the most votes overall can still lose the election because it depends on Electoral College votes, no matter how many people voted for them.

One big reason for the Electoral College is that it clearly picks a winner. This is supposed to make fewer fights about the popular vote, if electors vote how they should. Also, presidents need to think about different needs in different areas because no one area decides the election. How it affects swing states, by giving all votes to the winner, is also something people mention. Supporters also say bigger states are better because they have more elected officials, which means candidates pay attention to what they need. In the end, this makes it less likely that no candidate will get enough votes, so there are no run-offs needed.

Even with its good sides, the Electoral College isn't perfect. Still, some people like it best for picking the president. They warn against only using the popular vote, wondering if voters know enough. Even though there are rules for elections, those who like the Electoral College say it's best for the country. It's important to think about why people defend it.

Respected Senator, I have a question for you: What if we changed how we pick presidents? Let's talk about getting rid of the Electoral College and just counting everyone's votes. Shouldn't the people, not a few electors, decide who leads us?

Many important folks, like Nixon, Carter, Dole, and even the Chamber of Commerce, want to ditch the Electoral College. People worry that electors could change the election based on what they think. Doesn't that seem wrong?

Remember when Obama beat Romney? Obama won big in the Electoral College, with 29 more votes than Romney. This shows how unfair the system is and how it can stop the people's real choice. A popular vote? Fair for all!

Sure, the Electoral College can help when no one wins by a lot, like when Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92 got only 43% of the vote. It stopped things from getting crazy. But it gives too much power to things other than the popular vote. I think the American people should be in charge.

Thank you, Senator, for thinking about this: Should we keep the Electoral College, or should we switch to a system where the popular vote wins?

Dear Senator,

While some champion the Electoral College, claiming it forces candidates to engage with swing states and smaller populations (Source 3, "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing a president"), I strongly disagree. I believe it's a flawed and unfair way to pick our leader.

Source 2, "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong," clearly shows how unfair it is to individual voters. The winner-take-all approach in most states makes candidates focus on close races in "swing" states, ignoring other areas. A candidate could even lose the popular vote but win the state because of the Electoral College. Voters might not even know the victor's real views until they take office! This suggests the winner might not truly care about the state. Source 3 says, "The Electoral College requires a presidential candidate to have transregional appeal. No region has enough electoral votes to elect a president. So a solid regional favorite, such as Rodney in the South, has no incentive to campaign heavily in those states, for he gains no electoral votes by increasing his plurality in states that he knows he will win." For instance, if Mitt Romney faced Barack Obama, Obama's strength in the North would hurt Romney, which is, strangely, what the Electoral College wants.

So, I still think the Electoral College is bad and unfair. Source 2 backs this up, stating, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality." Basically, the Electoral College goes against modern democracy and might just be broken.

A lot of folks don't like the Electoral College, saying it's just not fair and we should get rid of it. Instead of everyone voting directly for the president, we pick "electors" who then make the call based on how everyone voted. Some people think this means our leaders aren't really chosen by us, the people. You can actually win the whole thing without getting the most votes, since those electors are supposed to vote how we want anyway. It strikes many as a pretty weird setup, so they're all for scrapping the whole thing.

Plus, there's the argument that a lot of voters just don't know enough about the electors they're backing. If you live in certain states, you don't see as many campaign ads or news stories, which makes it tough to make smart choices. Presidential candidates usually spend their time in swing states, trying to get noticed and get support. Sure, a president should try to connect with everyone, but this focus can make voters in states that don't matter as much feel like their vote doesn't count. Even though you're voting for a candidate, you're really choosing electors. And winning the popular vote doesn't mean you'll be president, which makes people want a more straightforward way of voting.

Political parties pick the "electors" who promise to vote for their person. It doesn't happen often, but the Electoral College winner can lose the popular vote. Remember in 2000, Gore won the vote, but Bush won the election – the first time that had happened since 1888. It could happen again, changing things even if most people voted differently. It causes a lot of arguments, because most states give all their electoral votes to whoever wins that state. But, it also stops situations where no one gets a majority, meaning we'd have to have more elections. The Electoral College makes sure we get a clear winner. People want their votes to matter, but that's not always how it goes.

A lot of people see the Electoral College as a confusing way to pick our leaders. It was supposed to get more people involved in voting, but it can actually hurt democracy by making individual votes less valuable. In a country that's all about voting, making informed decisions is super important. The system should make sure everyone knows their vote counts.

Dear State Senator, I’m writing to say I really think we should keep the Electoral College just as it is. Changing it to a popular vote system for picking presidents doesn't sound right to me. It's important because the House of Representatives gets to help choose the president, which makes things feel more just and less like a mess. Plus, it helps everyone understand how we actually pick our president.

The fact that the House of Representatives gets involved is a huge win for the Electoral College. Source One, in the third paragraph, says that each state gets electors based on how many people it has in Congress: one for each House member, plus two for the Senators. This way, lots of people get to have their say. Source One, in the fifth paragraph, points out that "The candidate's political party usually picks the electors," which shows that it's not just the House that's involved in elections. The government plays a really big role in the Electoral College because those votes matter a lot.

Another great thing about the Electoral College is that it's fair. Source Three explains that "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states have too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." This makes people feel better about how we choose the president. Source Three also says that "If everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states..." The Electoral College is a great deal, especially for the bigger states.

Most importantly, the Electoral College keeps us safe from election problems. Source Three, in paragraph twenty-two, highlights that "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets the most votes." This makes voting easier overall. The same source and paragraph also states that "We don't need more elections when no one wins the most votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." It guarantees that someone will win, which makes us trust elections more.

Of course, some people might not like the Electoral College if their candidate loses, but that's not a good enough reason to get rid of it. It keeps things orderly in presidential elections. Source Two, in paragraph Ten, says that "With the Electoral College, we don't vote directly for the president, but for electors, who then choose the president." Some think the Electoral College is unfair because they can't directly vote for who they want. But electors vote for their state's choice. Source Two also says, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only care about close races in 'swing' states." Even with these worries, the Electoral College does more than just count votes; it helps our government pick the best president.

So, I'm all for keeping the Electoral College. It's good for both regular people and the government. It makes elections fair, stops problems and confusion, and, most importantly, lowers problems so people are happier with how things turn out. Each year, the Electoral College makes elections clearer and more organized. Even with the government's big role, individual votes still matter a lot. Individual votes are key to the Electoral College, as are government votes.

Is the Electoral College really up to date? Back then, when tech wasn't so hot, it helped states count votes right. People went to vote for each state, and it worked fine. Now, tech's way better, so it feels slow. If you're a Republican in a place that's mostly Democrats, your vote feels like nothing. Maybe a vote for the country would be good; everyone could say their piece! Also, the winner-takes-all idea makes some states too big for their britches. Little states don't get enough news, so it's hard to vote.

This all started when the telegraph was a big deal! Back then, instead of states counting votes and sending people, they needed a new way. Imagine votes being lugged around on horses! Super risky! Papers vanish, people get hurt, some places get missed. The Electoral College made sense. But just like the telegraph, it's old news. Now, our phones can hold tons of votes. Computers are fast at math, giving us answers super quick. We send votes fast, so mistakes are fixed up fast. How about a computer to count it all? Each spot sends info to one computer, checked over and over. To make sure no one cheats, use a bunch of computers. What a waste that some folks won't let go of the old way! It's just so old!

It's just not right. A Democrat in a place that's all Republican votes, but the Republican wins. That Democrat's vote is like it never even happened. Poof. Gone. Every single time. But if we vote for the country, those votes would mean something. They would matter! The winner-takes-all thing makes it worse. You win California by a tiny bit, you get ALL their votes, ignoring half the people! That's no good. Big states win because they just have more votes. And the little states? Not so much. Politicians care more about the big guys, leaving the small ones out. Voters in places like the Dakotas might not even know, so they just pick a team. The big fights are in the big states. The ads are not always truthful, but voters catch on. Swing states get all the info. Small states? They're skipped, so voters don't know as much, and their votes might not matter at all. A vote for the country would mean people would go everywhere, no more big-state bias, and voting is fair for all.

So here's the deal: The Electoral College was okay way back when, but now? It's old, and it's not fair. We can talk and travel easy now, so it's just behind the times, a little crooked, doesn't work, and hurts how our democracy works. It's time for something new!

All over the world, folks are giving up their cars! Ditching cars makes sense because it cuts down on pollution and saves you money. People are really wanting to make a change or help their town! Countries such as China, Spain, and the U.S. are using cars less and less. While not everyone likes this, lots of people are happy because pollution is gross. Just think how much cleaner the air would be if everyone cut their car use in half. Cars are handy, sure, but they also cause a bunch of problems!

If you go to Vauban, Germany, you won't see many cars at all! You're allowed to own one, but parking can be a real pain and really expensive! Forty thousand dollars to park at home? Ouch! So, most people just leave their cars outside the area. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote a story called, "Life Goes On Without Cars in German Suburb." Seventy percent of families there don't own a car, and more than half sold theirs after they moved there. This shows why getting rid of cars can be a good and a bad thing. Saving money? Definitely! No car payments, no parking fees, and cheaper gas, too. And, the air is cleaner, to boot!

Paris, France, used to be super polluted, so they decided to ban certain cars from driving. Robert Duffer wrote that if your license plate ended with an even number, you had to stay home! Or, you had to pay a €22 fine. The next day, only odd numbers were allowed. Pollution went down! That's amazing for such a big city! Their goal? To have cleaner air so people could breathe easier. Duffer pointed out that "4,000 drivers were fined," and they "impounded 27 cars." Traffic went down by sixty percent! The ban was lifted as soon as the air got cleaner.

Bogota, Columbia, had a small idea that turned into something huge. Andrew Selsky wrote that "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It started out small, but now everyone loves it.

One day every year, no one is allowed to drive cars! But, of course, buses and taxis are okay. Seven million people are trying to breathe a little easier. They want cleaner transportation and less pollution. If you break the rules, you get a fine! Selsky says that Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" That is a crazy amount of bikes. Parks got better, sports became more eco-friendly, and the air got fresher. Now, other cities are starting to do the same thing.

Rosenthal also wrote about the U.S., saying that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. That helps "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. And what about New York City? You can always find a taxi or a subway! Parking is terrible, so people just don't bother with cars. Just imagine how much money they save! Traffic is still pretty bad, but just think about how much worse it would be with even more cars. The city really relies on public transportation. But, it can be expensive, and without it, the air would be a lot worse.

So, fewer cars? That's great news for everyone! It helps fight pollution, cleans up the air, and saves people money. Saying goodbye to your car is a win-win for everyone! If you can walk, do it! It's good for your health, your wallet, and the whole planet. Cars are fast, but you've got to think about how much they cost you and how much they pollute the air. Try going car-free for a while! See what happens, and have fun with it.

The car business is booming, and companies are swimming in cash! But, maybe we should drive less around our homes. Studies actually show that driving too much makes us stressed. Carlos Arturo Plaza pointed out in Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," that less driving helps people chill out. Driving less helps you, and it REALLY helps everyone! Lots of Americans drive, but fewer folks are actually buying cars. Maybe they're thinking about carpooling or something. Yeah, the bus might be slow, but it's still a good idea. Cutting back on driving where we live could lead to some seriously good things.

Cars are super polluting, which messes up the air and the ozone. We love how easy cars make life, and getting your license at 16 is awesome. But, we often forget how annoying it is to own a car, like how you NEED gas. Imagine how much better things would be if we didn't drive as much. Less driving would make the ozone layer happy, and we could all breathe easier. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," shows how well these bans work. Paris is known for being smoggy, so they stopped people from driving to clean up the air. Cold nights and warm days trap all the gross car fumes, making pollution worse, which is why they banned cars. After the ban, Paris's air was way better. The article even said, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 ,micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

We're not saying ditch your car completely, but maybe think about the bad stuff they do. You might find the bad outweighs the good. Vauban, Germany gets it, and they've changed how they live. Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," talks about how the town lives without cars. People there say they're happier because there's no traffic or accidents. They did it by getting rid of street parking, driveways, and garages – because who needs them without cars? Instead, Vauban made better sidewalks and easier ways to get around. One Vauban resident, who trains people in media and has two kids, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Before, the government spent 80 percent of its money on highways and only 20 percent on other ways to get around. But, that's changing in the next six years, which means better planning.

So, we really need to think hard about how we get around cities. Driving less has a bunch of good stuff that comes with it: the government can spend money on other things, the air is cleaner, we feel better, and we save money. We have to ask ourselves if driving all the time is worth all the problems it causes.

America's full of cars. People dig the quick rides, but walking? Not so much. Thing is, everyone driving makes roads a mess, total gridlock! The bummer? Cars mess up our planet and dirty the air we breathe. So, fewer cars? Awesome! It's a win for nature and for us.

Cutting back on cars? It's a total victory! We get a healthier planet and feel amazing. One report even said, "Cars are behind 12% of Europe's pollution... sometimes a whopping 50% in the U.S.!" Since cars spit out those gases directly, carpooling or hopping on the bus really helps. Plus, walking or biking? Keeps us in shape!

Smog, that gross city air, comes straight from cars. It's packed with nasty stuff that makes you sick and makes asthma way worse, especially in cities. Beijing, China, is a prime example of smog city. "Paris banned cars to clean up the air." Yep, Paris did that to fix the problem. Fewer cars are like hitting a "clean air" button.

But wait, there's more! Cars mess with water and land, too. They guzzle gasoline from the ground. Digging for that gas? It can hurt nature bad, causing oil spills that kill sea creatures. So, take trains, buses, or walk/bike! Then, we don't need as much gas. Cars use tons of fuel, so fewer cars mean way less fuel needed.

People say life's easier and chill without a car. No traffic jams or accidents! At a "Day Without Cars" thing in Bogota, Colombia, Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "'Less stress and cleaner air rock!'" See? Fewer cars mean less stress. Heidrun Walter said, "'I was stressed with a car. I'm happier now!'" She's happier in a German town where cars aren't a thing. Less stress? Another reason to ditch the car.

Some folks only need a car when buses/trains are a joke. Someone pointed out, "They walk, bus, or share cars," and "'A car is just for the tough trips.'" By dodging pointless car trips, we can build a better system where we don't need cars much, and other ways to get around get better. Better buses? Fewer cars, good for the planet and us.

Fewer cars? It's good for the Earth and chills you out. One source said, "Cars are the second-biggest U.S. polluter!" By skipping drives and using buses, we can slash tons of pollution in America. Less pollution, healthier people, and less stress? All from fewer cars!

More folks are realizing it's smart to drive less. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, a place with hardly any cars, in her article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." Heidrun Walter, who lives there, says it's way nicer without cars. Instead of car noise, you hear kids playing and bikes going by! The streets feel alive, and it's quiet and clean without all those cars.

Cutting back on cars could really make suburbs better places. Rosenthal's article mentions how cities have been getting denser and easier to walk around for twenty years. The goal is to make suburbs smaller, with great buses and trains, and shops you can easily walk to, so you don't need a car as much. Even the EPA thinks this is a good idea!

Less smog and air pollution are big wins when people drive less. Robert Duffer wrote "Paris bans driving due to smog," about how Paris tried to fight pollution by banning certain cars. It worked! Traffic went down 60% after five smoggy days. Pollution got closer to Beijing, China levels, and that city is known for bad air! Paris showed that less driving means less pollution and traffic.

Andrew Selsky's article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota" gives even more reasons to drive less. It's about Bogota's car-free day, which encourages people to try other ways to get around and cut down on smog. Because of it, they've built new parks, sports places, better sidewalks, restaurants, and fancy shops. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a business owner, says riding a bike with his wife is less stressful and better for the air.

Basically, driving less is great! Less smog, less pollution, and easier access to local shops. It makes life better now and helps make sure the planet has a good future.

The wheel was a big deal, leading straight to cars. Now, factories churn out tons of them each year, making it easier to zip around town and the globe. But here's the rub: gas-powered cars spew out carbon dioxide. This stuff is bad news for the planet, messing with the ozone and causing acid rain. Plus, too many cars lead to traffic jams and other headaches. And let's not forget the money pit that car ownership can be. To save our Earth, we should probably drive less and build fewer cars.

Cars are a major source of pollution, pumping carbon dioxide into the air and warming the planet. As it says in Article 1, paragraph 5, "Cars create 12% of the greenhouse gasses in Europe... and can be as high as half in the US". So, cutting back on car production and usage could really help the Earth. Less global warming means ice regions will stick around longer, keeping animals like penguins and polar bears safe and sound.

Traffic jams are the worst because they can pop up anytime. In Passage 2, paragraph 14, they noted, "traffic went down 60% in Paris" by limiting certain license plates on certain days. Less traffic makes getting around so much easier! That awful three-hour wait can turn into a quick twenty-minute drive, which gives people more time for, well, anything else. Plus, fewer cars and less traffic can only mean fewer accidents.

Paying for a car, whether you lease or buy, can empty your bank account fast. People have been in debt since the dawn of money, and it can happen to anyone. As Article 4, paragraph 32, points out, "Americans without cash couldn't get new cars, and the unemployed weren't driving to work anyway". Overspending on cars hurts both your wallet and the economy as a whole. Reducing car debt could be a real boost for the nation.

Bottom line, there are tons of good reasons to drive less. Gas-guzzling cars are wrecking the planet, melting ice, damaging the ozone, and causing extreme weather. Less traffic makes getting around easier and frees up your time. And cutting back on car production and leasing can give the economy a hand. Simply put, fewer cars are a win for everyone.

Okay, so, the Electoral College. It’s an old thing, but folks get upset about it. So, here’s how it goes: You don’t directly pick the President. You pick people called "electors," but heads up—they could change their vote! Remember the year 2000? The Electoral College put someone in office that most voters didn't even want! Some claim it stops ties, but that’s a weak reason.

This old way of doing things causes issues, like back in 2000. That election was a total mess! Ditching it would stop similar messes in the future. Way back in 1960, some nasty racists almost got Democratic electors in Louisiana to not vote for Kennedy. Electors can switch their votes, which they shouldn't. It's not common, but it's still a HUGE deal.

It's not just strange; the Electoral College is also really not fair. Each state gets at least one vote, so someone in Wyoming (tiny population) has the same power as all of California's representatives (big population!). Some say ties don't happen, but think back to 1979. Just a bit of votes in Ohio could have tied the election! Because of "winner-take-all," candidates don't care about tons of states. Some states in 2000 didn't even see visits or ads, so voters couldn't pick who to support or reject.

Tons of people feel left out, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but still lost in 2000. People might skip voting if they think their state is already decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. But don't think folks just want to brag instead of having their vote count. As we said, every vote is important. Popular vote might cause more ties, but it's still better than this old, bad system. Over 60 percent of voters don't like the Electoral College – that's a HUGE number! We need to make changes before things get worse.

It's very clear: We've seen failures, close calls, big risks, and unfairness. Electoral College fans are wrong. The election in 2000 and all the angry voters show how crazy it is to keep it. This system has been here for a long time, and it probably won’t disappear quickly. But now that more people see the problems, we can make things better.

Evidence suggests reducing car use leads to excellent outcomes. Air becomes cleaner. Fresh travel options pop up. Communities may even become safer.

Firstly, with fewer cars on roads, air pollution lessens. This is good for health and the planet. Vauban, Germany, is a great example! Studies say cars cause a lot of greenhouse gases. In Europe, cars create about 12% of these emissions. But in the U.S., where people love cars, it's a huge 50%! So, cities such as Vauban, Paris, and Bogota are trying hard to cut car emissions and make the environment cleaner. Experts generally think this trend will reduce driving for a long time, which is great news for carbon emissions. Transport is the second biggest source of pollution in the U.S.! Secondly, less driving means more fun ways to travel. Instead of only driving, people can walk in parks, play sports, and get fit. Plus, fewer cars mean less traffic. Bogota, Colombia, had a day without cars. People walked, biked, skated, or used public transport. The streets were much clearer! Paris even banned some cars to improve air quality, and traffic dropped by 60%! During these times, public transport was free from Friday to Monday, showing that less car use is good for everyone.

Heidrun Walter, who chooses not to have a car, says she is happier without driving. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, believes cutting car use reduces stress and cleans the air. Also, less driving makes things safer. M. Sivak's research shows fewer young people (16 to 39) are getting licenses, while older people are keeping theirs. One study found a 23% drop in driving among young people between 2001 and 2009. This change in travel leads to big improvements, making it a win-win situation. Miles driven are almost 9% less than the highest point, and fewer people own cars now compared to before the recession. Therefore, because of this change in who is driving, people might not go back to relying on cars as much as they did before.

In short, reducing driving can help the environment, create more travel choices, and make communities safer.

Okay, so traffic is a huge headache, right? Seriously, it impacts all of us, whether you're behind the wheel or not. For ages, cars were the default choice for everyone. But now, with all the pollution, fewer folks are driving, get it? Cutting back on car use would be amazing: less stress, fresher air, and a general feeling of being better!

Imagine how much chiller we could be, no more endless traffic jams or constant rushing. We could hop on a bike, bus, train, or just walk! Take Vauban, Germany, which is mostly car-free, according to a study. Apparently, their residents are way more relaxed since they can walk everywhere.

Plus, cars are terrible for the planet and totally trash the air. In Europe, cars account for roughly 12% of all global warming emissions! In the US, some super car-dependent areas pump out a whopping 50%! Paris is a perfect example of how bad cars are. Their air got as nasty as Beijing's, one study showed. They even had to temporarily ban cars to clean up the air!

Less car use is a win-win for the planet and us. Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day every year, another study says. This makes a huge difference in air quality, and people love it! Even when it rains, people show up for bike races and stuff. It worked so well that two other Colombian cities are copying the idea. Bogota even has the longest network of bike paths in Latin America, like 118 miles! Besides what the government is doing, people are driving less on their own. Apparently, driving is down by 9%. That might be because more people are working from home, so they don't need to drive as much.

Basically, things are changing, and maybe for the best. We're not as obsessed with cars anymore, which means less pollution, less stress, and a better life for everyone!

In the British system, the party with the most seats picks their leader. But in a popular vote, everyone votes. Direct democracy differs from the British system. Leaders can be good at gaining support but be bad, so it’s important to stop power misuse in elections. To protect freedom and ensure true popular control, the U.S. should keep the Electoral College.

The U.S. has been a republic for a long time because of its wise founders. The Electoral College came from Britain and the Iroquois, but the latter is often forgotten because of biases. Like British lawmakers picking the Prime Minister, the U.S. founders sought a way to pick their leader. They feared a leader elected directly might become tyrannical, so they made sure the President listened to Congress. Congress can remove a President for doing wrong. The Electoral College is a safeguard against dictators. Hitler wasn't elected, and Mussolini gained power just by being popular. The Electoral College, with regular citizens, stops these things. It’s not perfect, but it's representative democracy. Richard A. Posner said it's undemocratic because electors, not people, vote. All governments mess up. Even with good intentions, some want to get rid of the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who quit because of scandal, wanted it gone. Critics like Bradford Plumer think it's unfair and old. But the Electoral College watches elections, ensuring fairness with electors for each state and its people. This shows representative voting.

The Electoral College represents everyone, made with thought by America's founders. Despite arguments, great presidents like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson were elected because of it. To guard against tyranny and keep power with the people, the U.S. must keep the Electoral College.

Senator, listen up: This whole Electoral College thing? It just feels so old. Shouldn't we, the people, get to pick the president ourselves? Seriously, the way it is now? Totally unfair and messed up. Life's already a pain, but a nation that's fair? That's what matters, especially with the country owing so much. And let's be real, Obama didn't exactly help. The fact that where you live changes how much your vote counts? Can't get behind that.

These electors? They've got way too much power, making our votes feel pointless. We vote for them, sure, but then they just do whatever they want anyway. It's a huge betrayal of trust in the government. Shouldn't we, the voters, get a say in whether this whole Electoral College thing sticks around? I bet a ton of people feel the same. And hey, I may be young, but you should still listen to what I'm saying.

Plumer had it right; we don't vote for president directly. Remember the 2000 election? Gore got more votes, but still lost. If we're all supposed to be equal, why do these electors get special treatment? It makes our votes feel like they don't even matter. If electors are calling the shots, then what's the point of voting at all?

Some people say it stops one group from taking over, but come on, that's not how it works. The winner-takes-all thing in each state decides everything. So, who are these electors, and why do we think they're so special? Okay, I'm young, but I'm not dumb. Why ignore what I have to say? Let's face it, America's built on a lie, not fairness. We think we're in charge, but it's the politicians and the Electoral College pulling the strings.

America's going downhill, and it's not going to stop unless we make some changes. Getting rid of the Electoral College would be a start. Why should you care what I think, being young? Because I know what it's like to be ignored, even when you're smart. America knows what it needs. We can't let a small group decide everything. Let us vote directly for president, just once. If it doesn't work, fine, go back to the old way. But we won't know unless we try.

Okay, get this: cars, right? Most folks don't give them a second thought. But hold on! They're actually super important. Sure, we see them all the time, but loads of people can't afford them or even drive. Take Vauban in Germany. They flat-out said NO to cars! They've got a train to Freiburg and roads around the town, but none inside. You *can* own a car, but parking? Forget about it. There's hardly any, and it's crazy expensive. Buying a spot in a garage way outside town costs an insane $40,000 EXTRA! So, guess what? Seventy percent of the people there DUMPED their cars. More than half SOLD them after moving in! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, nailed it: "I was always stressed with a car. This is WAY better." Vauban, since 2006, is showing us the future: suburbs WITHOUT cars. Now that's a smart idea!

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and she was totally right. Cars might be cool, but hello, AIR PROBLEMS! They're wrecking the environment! Paris got so smoggy, they had to BAN drivers for a while. One Monday? Even license plates had to STAY HOME. Or pay about 31 bucks. The next day, maybe odd numbers? The air was SO TERRIBLE because the weather trapped all the car fumes. Insane! Traffic dropped like crazy – 60% in just FIVE DAYS! The air was basically like Beijing. When they blocked half the cars, the air got better RIGHT AWAY. Odd numbers got to drive the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, had a NO-CAR DAY. Walk, bike, skate, bus – take your pick! No cars, PERIOD. And you know what that meant? ZERO ACCIDENTS! And get this – they do it EVERY YEAR. This was year THREE! They figured seven million people would use something else instead of a car. It rained, but who cared? People still showed up! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop a soul!" Everyone felt way less stressed, and the air was cleaner too. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked with his wife. "It's good for stress and pollution," he said.

So, in the US, cars are a HUGE deal. But…are we over them? Some folks think so. We're a giant place with tons of suburbs, so naturally, we love cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – it's all very American. But maybe we're starting to change our minds. By 2013, driving was down almost 8%. That's like going back to 1995! Money troubles didn't help, and fewer people needed to drive to work. But Michael Sivak at Michigan said something else was going on. "Car ownership was dropping BEFORE the economy tanked." Whoa. We need other ways to get around, so people don't just go back to driving like crazy. New York's bike rentals, tolls, car rentals? That's a good start. One study found that young people drove 23% LESS between 2001 and 2009!

People are pushing for fewer cars for lots of reasons: they want to be safer, save some cash, and help out the planet.

Driving is a weekly activity for many, myself included, with regular trips to educational institutions, athletic events, and social gatherings. While some champion driving for its convenience and accessibility, excessive driving poses significant problems. Reducing driving might seem challenging or inconsequential, yet its benefits are substantial: improved air quality, enhanced physical well-being, and greater psychological contentment. For these reasons, authorities encourage reduced vehicle use.

Back in 2014, Robert Duffer highlighted the severe air pollution in Beijing caused by automobiles (source 2, paragraph 14).
