So, yeah, hopefully you're thinking about this now. It's hard to just give something this important to someone else. We need to trust ourselves and make choices based on good information.

Okay, check this out: It's Friday, 5 PM, work is OVER! No crazy traffic jams or cars honking—just a chill ride back home. Nice, right? Well, many Europeans have this on a normal Tuesday. It just goes to show how good life can be when cars aren't everything.

Big win: you save money! Cars eat up your cash. Bang! Accidents, speeding tickets, and BAM! you're broke before you can say "insurance." Plus, ditching your car means bye-bye to gas costs. Gas might be cheap now, but it ALWAYS goes up. And if it's too pricey, you might skip fixing your car, which is a bad idea. A bus might be cheap, but driving? Six bucks just for gas! Or, how about a bike? Bikes are WAY cheaper than cars.

Also, pollution! Cars make tons of noise, light, and air pollution. Like it or not, it messes with the environment. The U.S. might not be the worst right now, but more cars could change that. Maybe no cars is a dream, but how about some limits? Like, only drive on certain days based on your license plate, or carpool with coworkers? Even small stuff helps.

Here's the thing: People in cities with fewer cars are happier! Less stress from traffic jams or fender benders. Seriously, driving can be awful with all the distractions. Those distractions cause accidents or just make you stressed. Heidrun Walter, a mom, put it perfectly: "Having a car stressed me out. Now I'm much happier!" She loved the busy streets, where bike bells and kids laughing were louder than cars. Imagine having everything close, so you can just chill and walk or bike with your kids. Sounds good, yeah?

So, how would fewer cars change your life? Could it make you healthier and happier? Worth thinking about, right? What if less car use made everything way better?

Folks hop into their cars daily for work, chores, or trips, often overlooking the greenhouse gases harming our Earth. This pollution poses a significant challenge, especially in urban areas. Consequently, nations and organizations have launched initiatives encouraging people to drive less. These efforts have greatly aided in lowering harmful emissions and improving conditions in general!

The greatest advantage of reduced driving is its impact on pollution and those dreaded greenhouse gases. By driving less, individuals can conserve fuel costs while benefiting the planet. Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights the substantial contribution of cars to greenhouse gases. While cars account for 12% of these gases in Europe, they shockingly contribute 50% in the US! These figures underscore the significant effect of cars on gas emissions. Imagine the positive impact if many cities had fewer cars. Comparing greenhouse gas levels in car-free areas to those in car-heavy cities would reveal a stark contrast, with far lower emissions where cars are absent.

Moreover, fewer cars can truly enhance cities. Businesses might relocate to more accessible areas if people reduce driving, attracting more patrons. Subsequently, related businesses might also decide to move nearby. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" illustrates this, stating, "Parks and sports centers have bloomed, uneven sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks, rush hour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up." When people drive less, cities can evolve for the better.

To sum it up, there are numerous compelling reasons to drive less! From purifying the air to revitalizing cities, reducing driving can bring about substantial positive change.

Cars and how we get around are super vital today, when things move so fast. Lots of families even have a bunch of cars; they almost feel like they can’t live without them. But even though cars help us out, they also bring a bunch of problems. If we didn't use our cars as much, we could save our planet and its stuff, which would really help our environment!

Most people drive carefully, sure, but some folks cause wrecks because they're wild behind the wheel. People break the rules of the road all the time, which can lead to horrible accidents. Driving without thinking is a big reason people die, especially young folks, and that makes you think about how safe driving really is. Sadly, tons of people die in car crashes every year. If we got rid of cars, these awful things could stop happening. If we walked, biked, took the bus or subway, we'd have fewer accidents and be healthier!

Less driving means cleaner air, for real! Cars make the air super dirty. Studies show that getting around is a big reason why the air in the United States is polluted. If we didn't depend on cars so much, the air would be way cleaner. Also, "cars carrying people make up 12% of the air pollution in Europe... and sometimes it's even 50% in places where there are tons of cars in the United States." Ditching cars would really cut down on pollution, which would make our future healthier.

Getting rid of cars also saves resources. Cars need gas and diesel, and we're running out of that fast. Other things need those resources too. If we didn't rely on cars as much, we wouldn't use up so much fuel, and we could save it for important stuff. This helps fix the environment and makes sure we have resources for the kids who come after us.

In the end, driving less would make the world way better. It protects what's coming and makes us worry less. We can't change what's already happened, but if we don't drive as much, we can stop things from getting worse and make sure things are safer later. It promises us cleaner air, safer lives, and a chance to be nice to the Earth.

Cars have massively changed everything, both good and bad. They make getting around easy, but also cause tons of crashes. Is the good worth the bad? People argue about that all the time! For 100 years, cars helped us get ahead. Now, we REALLY see how bad they are. Less driving could mean less yucky air, saving resources, and way fewer wrecks, right?

Lots of countries are trying different things to fight pollution. Some ban cars completely, others only let you drive certain days. For instance, Vauban, Germany, makes owning a car super expensive. So, most families (70%) don't have them! New people moving there even sell their cars before they arrive. More and more places are thinking about doing the same thing. Vauban, with about 5,500 people, shows it can work. People there say they're happier without cars! In Paris, France, they only let cars with certain license plates drive on certain days. It worked GREAT! Smog went down by 60% in just five days! Bogotá, Colombia, also wants to be green like Germany. Carlos Arturo Plaza says it's "a fantastic opportunity to reduce stress and air pollution." In Europe, cars cause 12% of the bad air and greenhouse gases. And in the US, less driving helps the Earth too. All over the world, countries are trying hard to protect the planet by cutting down on pollution.

For the last 100 years, cars have used up a HUGE amount of stuff that we can't replace, and it's hurt the environment. We're running out of that stuff FAST, and it takes forever to get more. Using it is bad because it puts carbon in the air, which makes the greenhouse effect worse. We HAVE to save resources to keep the air clean, don't you think?

Now, more and more people are saying we should ban or limit cars, mostly because so many people die in car accidents. Car crashes are a top killer in the US, and probably other places too. If banning cars could save a bunch of lives, we should think about it seriously. Fewer cars would mean fewer crashes, injuries, and deaths. Plus, governments could use the money they save on roads to make things better for people walking and build awesome playgrounds, which would make communities better.

The world is changing fast, so we need to change too, to protect the Earth and everyone on it. Banning cars could be one step towards a better future for everyone. It could cut down on greenhouse gases, save important resources, and make fewer people die in car accidents. Maybe, in a few years, cars will be a thing of the past.

It's not just about adding up votes to pick the president, is it? A better way? Simply counting each person's choice, plain and simple. Honestly, you can't deny this system is seriously behind the times.

The Electoral College? So not fair, right?! As source two points out, this "winner-takes-all" deal means candidates only care about those swing states. Talk about a letdown. Some states get all the love, while others? Nada.

And get this, the Electoral College is, like, ancient. Source two even brings up Hawaii's 1960 election to show how long it's been around. Hello? We need something new, a modern way to vote!

The whole reason for the Electoral College? Weak. Source two tells us we're not even voting directly for the president. Nope, we're voting for "electors." What?! Let's ditch this confusing thing and just vote directly.

Bottom line? The Electoral College needs to disappear. It's just not fair, it's old news, it makes no sense, and it's bad for democracy. Getting rid of it? That'd make way more people want to vote and get involved.

Trading vehicles for other options? A bit strange, eh? However, many individuals are embracing this shift. The reason for this choice? They are striving for enhanced well-being and increased contentment. As Heidrun Walter, as quoted in Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece on a German town, put it, "Driving? It's nothing but stress. Now? It's absolutely amazing." Similar to numerous residents of Vauban, Germany, she has discovered a more tranquil life without relying on a car. The town itself? Lacks driveways and garages entirely. This strongly motivates one to abandon the vehicle. Instead, the emphasis is on buses, bicycles, and pedestrian travel. Dismissing car-related stress, Vauban views owning a car as an inconvenience. Greater than half of the people abandoned their cars before settling there, with no desire to go back. Andrew Selsky, within "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," communicates Carlos Arturo Plaza's thoughts. While biking alongside his spouse, he mentioned, "It's delightfully relaxing!"

Furthermore, Plaza noted, "Cleaner air is a real benefit." Gradually, more folks are recognizing the pollution cars bring to the table. Paris, notoriously known for smog, has commenced a project to purify its atmosphere. Robert Duffer, from "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city," clarified how cars with even numbers were banned on select days, with odd numbers banned on alternative days. The outcome? Significant. Duffer reported, "Traffic? Dropped 60 percent within Paris after five smog-filled days." Elizabeth Rosenthal, in "The End of Car Culture," implies, "If this continues...the planet will profit, given that cars are leading pollutants in the USA." We Americans tend to consume excessively, causing vast amounts of pollution. We desire the latest devices, which come at a higher cost than simply currency. Greenhouse gasses are multiplying, so a change is needed. Rosenthal points out that "President Obama set some big goals to cut America's greenhouse gas emissions last week." Within car-free locations, the advantages extend beyond the air quality. Andrew Selsky wrote concerning "the construction of 118 miles of bicycle paths" and "rough sidewalks swapped for smooth ones" in Bogota, Colombia. This amplifies walking's allure. The writer shares that "officials from other countries came to Bogota to see carfree day and were pumped," including the mayor of Paraguay's capital. Noticing positive changes makes others wish to be involved. Columbia's event has inspired conversations nearby, having "two other Colombian cities, Calli and Valledupar, joined in." Despite this, car companies are developing. Rosenthal explains, "But it could hurt the car business. So, companies like Ford and Mercedes are calling themselves "mobility" companies that do more than just make cars." Since walking, biking, and buses are getting more attention, car businesses are trying to retain customers, even without their cars. She shares Bill Ford's idea of car dealerships "work with phone companies to build cities where 'walking, bikes, cars, and buses all work together to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer.'" The combination of transit with tech for mutual benefit.

Without question, transition is taking place. Cities are trialing novel transportation methods, or revitalizing the existing ones. Henry Ford may be upset that cars are fading in popularity; however, nature would be thrilled with our eco-friendly endeavors. The future isn't yet written; however, we have only one Earth, so let us protect it.

The clamor to abolish the Electoral College resonates deeply, echoing the electoral uncertainties of 2000. To many, its existence feels unjust, superfluous, and ripe for elimination.

However, a counterargument exists. Richard A. Posner highlighted its role in "preventing elections where no candidate secures a majority." It ensures a definitive outcome, preempting protracted and potentially turbulent runoff elections. Posner further observed that the victor typically amasses a greater share of Electoral College votes than the popular vote, mitigating the likelihood of intense disputes over the outcome compared to a simple tally of individual votes. Yet, the foundational principle remains: the populace should elect their president. Thus, the perception of the Electoral College as flawed and perplexing persists, fueling the call for its removal.

A significant concern arises from the possibility of the popular vote winner being denied the presidency, potentially depriving the nation of its preferred leader. Bradford Plumer contends that the Electoral College raises the specter of "the popular vote winner losing again." This disenfranchisement contravenes the principles of democracy. Plumer further points out that voters cannot always "control how their electors vote," diminishing their influence in electoral outcomes. This erosion of popular sovereignty strengthens the argument for the immediate abolition of the Electoral College.

The considerable authority wielded by electors is yet another point of contention. Plumer notes that "electors could always go against what the people want," underscoring their impact on presidential selection. This power rightfully belongs to the citizenry. He details how electors "have sometimes voted against their party and picked whoever they wanted," illustrating their capacity for independent action. Popular vote should be the sole determinant. The course of America should reflect the collective will of its people, expressed through the direct election of their president.

In conclusion, the Electoral College represents an inequitable system for Americans. The right to elect our leader should be inviolable. The popular vote should prevail, and the Electoral College should be consigned to history.

Each day finds countless individuals hopping into their automobiles, heading to their jobs or schools. But guess what? Things, they are a-changin'! A burgeoning trend involves driving less, and it's truly a win-win situation. It's not just beneficial for our planet; it also significantly enhances our relaxation levels.

Opting to drive less translates to fewer car fumes. How awesome is that for the environment? Car exhaust generates that awful smog, trapping unwanted air particles. Cities frequently become smog-choked because of this exhaust. Consider Paris, for example, which once suffered from an astounding 147 micrograms of airborne junk. After implementing car restrictions, the smog vanished quickly – in just one day, and this was not a recurring issue! Cars are prolific greenhouse gas producers: a significant 12% in Europe, and a whopping 50% in the US. This demonstrates cars' profound environmental impact. Even a single car-free day per week can dramatically curb atmospheric gas emissions. Even habits of Americans are evolving! Fewer people are buying cars or driving. Since '95, individual mileage has decreased by nearly 9%. Continuing to abandon cars can substantially decrease emissions and considerably improve the environment.

Beyond environmental benefits, ditching the daily commute greatly diminishes stress. In Vauban, Germany, car ownership is uncommon – just 30% of people own cars. Despite beliefs about the necessity of minivans for families, Heidrun Walter prefers a car-free existence. She previously stated, "When I had a car, I felt incredibly stressed. I am much happier and relaxed now." For three years consecutively, Bogota, Colombia, has observed a "Day Without Cars." Only taxis and buses are permitted. Even during inclement weather, residents love this day. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza also said that, "It's a great way to chill out and clean up the air for everyone." Now two other Columbian cities want to start the same tradition. And guess what? Technology facilitates remote work, virtual social connections, and the ability to avoid commutes. It can be a major stress reducer!

We once considered cars the superior transportation method. But, we didn't always acknowledge their impact on the planet or our own well-being. It's time to reassess our habits and gain a more comprehensive understanding.

The Electoral College, huh? What a terrible way to pick the president! It screws voters. State electors sometimes go rogue. Remember Al Gore? Won the most votes, still lost. Nope. This system is ancient.

When you vote, you pick "electors." They should vote for the winner but can do whatever. Someone can win the popular vote and still lose! How? Not enough electoral votes from each state. Totally unfair! The people's choice should win, period. Electors used to be smart to pick for the dumb. Now? Anyone can pick a good leader. Gore lost in 2000, most votes or not. Proof it's a bad system!

Why is the Electoral College so bad? Winner-take-all. Win a state, even by a little, you get ALL the votes. Candidates ignore small states or sure-win states. They focus on big swing states like Ohio, which changes its mind. Smart? In 2000, 17 states got ignored! Voters forgotten, clueless. In close races, half the voters don't count. In California, almost half of the 35 million voters don't matter! Elections shouldn't depend on Ohio while everyone else is ignored.

We need a better system. Only a national popular vote guarantees the most popular person wins. Candidates would have to listen to everyone, even rural voters! American citizens deserve a fair election that makes most people happy.

In the United States, we use the Electoral College to elect presidents, a system created by the nation's founders. When you vote, you're actually choosing electors. These electors then cast votes for the president and vice president. Congress tallies all the electoral votes. Those who support it believe it guarantees a clear outcome. Others aren't fans. They argue it favors larger states, can lead to unusual results, and the winner-take-all approach seems unfair.

The number of electors each state gets depends on its representation in Congress, meaning bigger states have more clout. For instance, Hawaii has a mere four electors, while Texas boasts thirty-eight! Therefore, candidates focus on densely populated areas. As Bradford Plumer put it, tiny Wyoming has "only one vote. One person from Wyoming with 500,000 voters has equal power as 55 people in California who stand for 35 million voters. What Congress chooses may not reflect the public's genuine will."

Our current presidential election process can be complicated. Back in 2000, Gore won the popular vote but still lost. Can we really trust electors to stay true to their pledges? A changed elector vote could shift the election's outcome. There have also been other oddities, like Hawaii having two sets of electors in 1960.

The winner-take-all aspect of the Electoral College is a major issue. Getting the most votes in a state means grabbing all its electors. This leads candidates to ignore states they deem unwinnable, focusing instead on "swing states." According to Richard A. Posner, "In 2000, 17 states didn't see candidates, including Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in 25 major media markets didn't see a single ad."

Therefore, the Electoral College has flaws. It generates problems and feels unfair to voters. Even figures like Nixon and Carter wanted it abolished. Candidates over-invest in certain states, electors may break promises, and the winner-take-all system is unjust. The Electoral College complicates matters.

Wow, I feel so light! Can you believe it? My car is *gone*! I know, I know, everyone has a car. And basically everyone wants one, right? Getting rid of a car seems scary. But what if it's not so bad? What if things are changing? Maybe this isn’t such a bad thing!

Have you heard of Vauban, Germany? People *love* living there without cars! Can you believe that about 70% of families there don’t even own a car? Get this – over half, like 57%, *sold* their cars just to move there! Okay, it's just Vauban mostly, but still, it's growing. Elisabeth Rosenthal says cars cause a whopping 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases. But in places that are *crazy* for cars, like the U.S., it could be as high as 50%! Robert Duffer said Paris made its air cleaner fast by banning cars. Less driving, less pollution, a healthier planet – awesome, right? Imagine more places like Vauban!

Even Bogota, Colombia, has a special day that makes it fun to drive less! On that day, people just walk, bike, skate, or take the bus. The mayor says they’ve been doing it for three years, no matter what the weather is like! Other cities in Colombia, like Cali and Valledupar, are starting to do the same thing. This could get really big! Maybe it will even become a holiday someday. And less cars means more walking, which means governments might build better sidewalks and paths. We could see new restaurants, parks, and shops for walkers, which would make life better! If we all did this, we would find new and fun things to do, and benefit from driving less.

Elisabeth Rosenthal thinks we might drive less in the U.S. soon too. The numbers show fewer people are buying cars, driving, or getting licenses lately. Then we might end up chatting on the bus and making some new friends! Driving less might even make us friends!

From cleaner air to a better way of living, and yes, even new friends, driving less has so many cool benefits!

Lately, it feels like fewer folks are behind the wheel. Instead, many share rides or discover new ways to travel. For example, Elisabeth Rosenthal tells us about Vauban, Germany, where cars are almost forbidden, drastically cutting down car use. Robert Duffer also highlights the serious pollution issues in Paris and the interesting solutions they're trying. And Andrew Selsky reported on a neat project in Colombia where people experiment with different ways to travel without driving. The best part? Less driving means cleaner air, less traffic, and safer roads.

Cars really pump out a ton of pollution. Robert Duffer notes that in France, "Diesels are 67 percent of cars..." (Duffer 16). The reason? The government taxes diesel less. Yet, diesel harms the environment more than gasoline, worsening pollution. Cutting back on these fuels could greatly clean up the air. Also, cars cause around 12 percent of greenhouse gases in Europe. However, in some parts of the U.S., it's a shocking 50 percent (Rosenthal 5). This highlights how much cars contribute to pollution in the United States. Luckily, using trains or carpooling can significantly reduce this.

More cars simply equal more traffic jams. Rosenthal even states, "New York's bikesharing and rising bridge tolls show these new goals.(Rosenthal 37)" The constant stream of cars, for work and daily life, means toll bridges rake in a lot of cash. Carpooling offers a great way to use toll bridges less. Sharing rides with coworkers can genuinely ease traffic congestion.

Moreover, public transport, like the BART in San Francisco, has greatly reduced the number of drivers. Sure, public transport might take longer, but it's a way better alternative for getting around. (Rosenthal 40) This lowers the number of cars on the road. With fewer cars, it's easier for everyone to travel.

Safety is a major reason to cut back on driving, too. In Colombia, they have a car-free day each year where everyone walks or bikes. (Selsky 20) This means fewer cars and way less risk of car crashes. If we discover other ways to travel, there will be fewer accidents. While many people need a car, safety is vital. Also, new tech helps people drive less. Telecommunication lets people chat with friends and family from afar. (Rosenthal 35) Therefore, people do not need to travel as much. Texting and social media help people stay connected, meaning less trouble.

In conclusion: cars cause numerous issues like accidents, terrible traffic, and awful pollution. By driving less, we can address these problems and create a safer, healthier place for everyone.

Okay, Electoral College? Well, people vote for electors who vote for President, then Congress counts it all up. But is *that* really better than just, like, counting every single person's vote? That's what everyone's yelling about. Even though it's been around forever, the whole thing's a bit clunky. A straight-up popular vote, though? Seems way fairer, and way less chance of things ending in a total deadlock. Makes more sense, right? You know?

Letting go of your car? Sounds hard, right? Problems might pop up. But wait! There are lots of good things, too. Think about cleaner air – everyone wins! And guess what? Choosing other ways to get around could save you some serious cash.

Using our cars less can really cut down on pollution. Cars make up around half of all emissions in the U.S., Elisabeth Rosenthal writes in part five. Sounds like a lot, but some cities are fighting back. Take Paris, for example. The smog was terrible! But by stopping certain cars on some days, the smog disappeared super fast. Traffic went down by a huge 60 percent! If Paris did it with a small change, other cities can clean up, too.

Another awesome thing about driving less? Saving money! Buying a car can cost a fortune. Plus, Rosenthal mentions in paragraph twenty-nine that bridges and tunnels make driving even pricier. But bikes? They're cheap! And you can ride them on paths for free. Some people might think switching to bikes is crazy, but it’s already happening in Bogota, Colombia. Once a year, almost all cars can't drive on the roads. Selsky says in paragraph twenty that millions join in, rain or shine! This shows that anyone can use different transportation.

So, to sum it up, using cars less has tons of benefits. You get cleaner air and maybe more money. Small, daily changes can make a big difference for everyone.

Every day, we drive, but have you ever considered the problems cars cause, like stress and bad air? What if we didn't use them as much? Less stress, safer streets, and cleaner air! It’s good for Earth and keeps our families safe from car dangers.

Traffic is super stressful, especially when you're in a hurry. Fewer cars mean less traffic, don't you think? Andrew Selsky noted it's "a great chance to cut stress." Nobody likes traffic jams; they're annoying and make you mad. Stress is bad for you. Driving stress is very harmful.

Cars are useful, but they're also dangerous! People make cars, and people mess up! Some mistakes are small, but others are deadly. Many people have died in car crashes, and more have lost loved ones. Drive less, and there are fewer crashes; this makes our world safer for families.

Elisabeth Rosenthal said transportation is the second-biggest polluter in the U.S., after power plants. We often ignore pollution, but it’s a big issue. It’s stealing our clean air! Power plants and car pollution are quickly dirtying our air. Drive less, and we clean the air. It’s great for the environment and gives future generations better air.

Because there are so many benefits to driving less, we could copy Europe and Colombia. Selsky talked about Colombia's "Day Without Cars," which they've done for three years! Germany has a car-free suburb, showing us a different way of life. Paris has also had car-free days, which cleaned the air, and people were happier. Cars are convenient, but are they worth all the problems they create?

Vehicles? You see them all over the place, right? They're nearly impossible to dodge! Got to get somewhere? Using a car is the usual way. But check this out: public transportation is making a comeback! And get this: it really rocks! Less smog, fewer autos causing gridlock, plus it gives cities a boost! Back then, cleaner cities were like a fantasy. But now? It is spreading fast!

Consider cities like Chicago or New York! Remember how murky the air used to be? Cars add a lot to that! Drive less, and bang! Cleaner air and happier people! To fight terrible pollution, Paris even stopped some cars. The smog was just crazy! Less use of cars reduces that mess, not just for Paris, but all over Earth.

Traffic? Really a drag! Less driving means better streets and safer roads. Case in point? Paris banned cars! Traffic crashed—down a huge 60%! — even though smog lingered. Fewer cars also mean fewer wrecks! Chicago highways are getting smoother. Less traffic helps roads last longer and makes it easier to maintain them. Car-Free Day proves this! Think about that across a country as big as the U.S.! Less driving is a major win for everyone!

Public transport used to seem unreal. But these days? Businesses, even entire cities, are seriously cutting back on cars. Less car action in busy areas? That is awesome! New sidewalks, cool restaurants, springing up everywhere—big pluses! Instead of constantly fixing damaged roads, cities can put that money into awesome stuff, like parks and sports fields! Plus, even more stores and restaurants! Cities thrive with public transport and less dependence on cars!

Cars? Sure, they've been the go-to thing. But believe it or not, cutting back on cars is doable with less smog, less traffic, and better cities. Giving up cars seems hard, but things are starting to shift! Big cities are adding public transport to smaller towns. The good of less smog is better than small problems. Smart ideas make it possible to ditch cars in the suburbs. It encourages smart development and fixes. Smart ideas help cut car use, building a brighter and safer future!

Okay, so many folks really dislike driving. Increasingly, nations are limiting, or even stopping, car use. Guess what? This might not be the worst idea.

Cars definitely spew out some serious emissions. Therefore, less driving means reduced air pollution. It is a beginning to prevent us from completely ruining the Earth. Even though pollution exists already, little actions matter significantly! Consider the savings - no car bills, less gas cost. Use that money for leisure! We could find other commuting options.

Sociologists also think fewer cars could benefit the planet and decrease pollution. One stated, "After power plants, driving causes the most pollution in the US!" In essence: fewer cars = healthier planet = happier people.

Another crucial aspect involves money! People could keep the money spent on gas, repairs, and vehicle purchases, particularly with uncertain jobs. This saved income? People can spend it on life’s essentials!

Frankly, car usage is not always required. Teens, for instance, drive to socialize or get to their jobs. However, alternatives exist! I'm not suggesting total car abandonment, but cutting unnecessary trips. Walk when possible!

Elisabeth Rosenthal, the author of "The End of Car Culture," noted that her kids, ages 19 and 21, never bothered obtaining licenses! They are thriving! According to her, "They get to their summer jobs and hang out by walking, buses, or carpooling with friends." Sharing rides is feasible, reducing both traffic and wasted money.

Essentially, reducing car dependency offers numerous advantages. I suspect EVEN MORE benefits are on the horizon!

Okay, so how about you try to drive less? It's a win-win for both you and the Earth!

For real, it's actually beneficial for you too. Think of not driving as a weight off your shoulders. One family from Germany even said, "Owning a car was a hassle. We feel much freer now!" And it's not just about stress reduction. You also end up saving money on gas and might even improve your health. Why not walk or bike instead of hopping in the car? Take a look at Bogota's "Car-Free Day." Suddenly, parks, sports areas, nicer sidewalks, and cool shops started popping up. One study even called it "an opportunity to chill out." It's just one of the many upsides to cutting back on driving!

And hey, let's not forget the bigger picture here. Driving less makes a real difference for our planet. Experts are clear: cars cause pollution. In fact, they say, "Cars are responsible for 12% of Europe's total pollution!" So, logically, fewer cars mean cleaner air for everyone. Paris, a city known for its smog, experimented with alternating license plates. Sure, some people complained, but it resulted in less gas consumption. Columbia even has an annual car-free day where only buses and taxis are allowed, "to encourage people to explore alternative ways to get around and reduce air pollution." We can all pitch in! Even in the US, there's a trend of fewer people buying cars, driving as frequently, or even getting driver's licenses, which shows a shift in how people view cars. Some might grumble, but the planet benefits. Happier people, healthier planet? These are all great reasons to drive less.

Every four years, America votes. It's on the Tuesday right after November's first Monday, which is Election Day. But, it's more than just choosing a President! It's tricky! Our democracy uses the Electoral College, which complicates things. Electors decide who will be President and Vice President. Then Congress counts up their choices. Usually, these electors are chosen by political groups. But is this system really the best way? I believe we could do better.

We don't get to directly choose our President. Instead, our votes go to the Electoral College, who pick the electors. It's them, not us, that makes the final call. As "What Is the Electoral College?" explains, when you vote, you're actually supporting electors who back a candidate. Almost anyone can be an elector, unless they're already in office. Their decision is final. It's not that simple! "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" shows that over 60% of people want easier ways to vote. That means most people crave straightforward voting.

Electoral votes aren't divided up fairly. Larger states like California get far more votes than smaller ones like Vermont. While the House tries to make sure everyone has a voice, the Electoral College doesn't do the same. The system is unbalanced. This causes a big problem, as it doesn't match our ideals of fairness.

The Electoral College makes things confusing. Voters might be unsure who they're supporting, and they can't control what the electors do. Electors who go against what most voters want create problems. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" shows the problem. Voters select electors, and they pick the President, so the process isn't direct.

Some people argue that getting rid of the Electoral College could hurt our country and go against what America stands for. "In defense of the Electoral College..." suggests that parties pick electors who should support their candidate, but the person who gets the most votes overall might still lose. Even if you cast a ballot, your vote might not matter. It can stop runoff elections and helps if no one gets the majority, but there are still problems.

To sum up, the Electoral College isn't good for our country. It's hard to understand and feels a bit unfair. While it might have some good points, the bad ones outweigh them. It's uneven and could throw our country off balance. For a fairer America, we should scrap it.

Okay, so less driving is a big help, right? Heidrun Walter, a mom, felt calmer not driving so much. Parking can be crazy expensive! Imagine $40,000 to park at home! Cars also pollute a lot, about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, even more in the US – sometimes half! Less driving means walkable neighborhoods. Also, cleaner air is awesome for the planet. Hybrid cars are getting popular; they're cleaner. But weather matters: cold nights and hot days trap pollution, making air dirtier.

Paris had terrible car smog, so they made rules to fix it. Breaking the rules cost $31, and they might take your car! This majorly cut traffic, down by 60%! The air improved after the rules, so Paris let some cars back in, like odd license plates on Tuesdays. Carlos Arturo said biking with his wife is way less stressful, and Andrew Selsky says the air is cleaner. More cities like this idea; Bogota, Colombia, built over 118 miles of bike paths.

Asuncion, Paraguay's mayor, Enrique Rivera, saw other leaders visit Bogota because they loved the changes and wanted to copy them. By 2013, Americans drove about 9% less, like in 1995. The economy is part of it, with less money for cars, and jobless people drive less. If you can't afford a car, a bike seems better for work. But some people just hate biking or walking. Plus, cars cost a ton with gas and insurance.

So, good news: Americans might like cars less. As families grew, people drove less in 2005. Cars also make roads dangerous. There are fewer young drivers (16-39), and older folks keep their licenses longer. Studies show young people drove 23% less from 2001 to 2009. Bill Ford wants to team up with phone companies to make smart cities where walking, biking, cars, and buses all work together to be efficient, save resources, pollute less, and be safer. Some areas in Germany banned cars, and now 70% of families don't own cars, and 57% sold their cars before moving there. This stuff helps clean our air and protect the planet.

Vehicles are quite ubiquitous! These days, a lot of folks all over the world depend on them to get where they need to go. Indeed, they smooth out existence, transforming chores and excursions into less strenuous activities. Nonetheless, despite all this ease, occasionally we lose sight of the drawbacks. Yes, automobiles offer assistance, but being overly reliant on them can create complications that outweigh the benefits. Excessive car use can significantly harm the environment and create tension, potentially without our conscious knowledge. Therefore, we need to seriously reconsider the extent of our driving habits.

Our ecosystem furnishes crucial resources, like nourishment and the very air we inhale; however, cars create a significant hurdle. They discharge carbon dioxide, contaminating the atmosphere. Robert Duff, in his piece "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," mentions that Paris experienced such severe air pollution that driving restrictions had to be put in place. This was due to widespread diesel car usage encouraged by tax benefits. Duff emphasizes that "diesels constitute 67 percent of cars in France, contrasting with a.3 percent on average elsewhere in Western Europe, reports Reuters." Furthermore, Duff indicates that more populated cities typically have worse air quality. Due to the environmental harm caused by vehicles, efforts are being made to reduce their usage. Elisabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars" recounts the tale of Vauban, Germany, where inhabitants abstain from car ownership. In Vauban, 70% of families lack cars, and 57% give up their cars upon relocating there. Car ownership remains an option, but parking fees are prohibitively high, around 40,000. Residents opt for walking or cycling instead. Vauban exemplifies that minimizing car pollution is indeed achievable.

Aside from its impact on the environment, cars subtly induce stress. Heidrun Walter, as quoted in "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," expresses increased happiness without a vehicle: "When I had a car, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." Similarly, entrepreneur Carlos Arturo noted that a car-free day in Bogota offered "a great chance to lower stress and clean up the air." Vehicles can dominate our routines, and the consequences might not be apparent until we scale back our reliance on them.

People aren't as obsessed with cars as they were before. Cars used to be just a way to get around, but now, because of fancy technology, fewer people are driving, especially in cities. You might think less driving is a bad thing, but it's actually a good thing in many ways!

The United States has always loved cars. We made the Model T, and everyone remembers "Mustang Sally." Even President Obama wanted us to drive less to help the environment. In "The End of Car Culture," he mentioned that things were changing a lot. Studies show that people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not even getting their driver's licenses as much. By 2013, driving had decreased by almost 9% compared to the past. Also, fewer young people are getting licenses. This is because of the internet, which allows us to work from home, cities are improving, and we have convenient carpooling apps on our phones. Cars aren't completely useless, but life is easier than it was in the sixties because of technology. In the U.S., driving is more of a choice, but in places like Paris, France, there are rules about driving.

Paris, France, is a beautiful city, but it has a bad pollution problem. News headlines said, "Paris bans driving due to smog!" They stopped some people from driving to help reduce pollution. If you drive when you're not allowed to, you have to pay 22 euros. Only cars with certain license plates can drive on certain days. The article also said that traffic decreased by 60% after they did this! Some cars could still drive, like hybrids and cars with three or more people inside. Diesel cars, which are very popular in France, were the main cause of the pollution. Regular gas cars weren't as bad. Just like Paris, Bogota, Colombia is trying to improve its environment.

Colombia has a car-free day where no driving is allowed! It's a 24-hour ban, which is stricter than Paris' rules. If you drive, you'll pay 25. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" said that "millions of Colombians walk, bike, skate, or take buses to work during the carfree day." Like Paris, Bogota wants people to find other ways to travel and reduce pollution. A businessman named Carlos Arturo Plaza said it's "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Driving can be stressful, especially with road rage, which can ruin your day. Driving is okay sometimes, but there are other options. Colombia wants to "promote alternative transportation and reduce smog." Cars are becoming more advanced, but people aren't as excited about them anymore, even though they used to be very popular. Now, they're mostly used for getting from one place to another when you don't have other choices.

A huge plus to driving less? It's definitely that the air is way cleaner! Cities are choking on pollution from cars, especially when roads are super crowded. Take Paris – you hear stories about them banning cars some days because the air is just that bad. This gunk in the air makes climate change worse and messes with our lungs, too. When Paris had a really bad smog problem, they stopped cars from driving, and traffic actually dropped by a whopping 60 percent! The weather can mess with pollution, too; cold nights and warm days can make it even worse.

Also, skipping the car gets you moving! People end up walking or biking more, which is great exercise. You also get to chat with people and enjoy being outside. When more people walked around, gyms and parks got busier, and new stores and restaurants popped up, helping the local economy.

More and more, folks are trying out other ways to get around, like buses, bikes, or just walking. Studies show that fewer young people are driving – a big 23 percent drop from 2001 to 2009. If more of us used ride-sharing or other ways to travel, pollution would go down everywhere. Less exhaust from cars would for sure make the air better and help people breathe easier.

Think about it: all the money spent on roads and traffic could go to better things if we didn't rely so much on cars. Sure, lots of people drive, but there are always other options. And when you drive less, you save money, especially since gas prices jump around so much.

Basically, there are tons of great reasons to cut back on or ditch the car. Not only does it help the environment with cleaner air, but it's also good for you. Walk, bike, or take the bus – it's a cheap way to get around! Plus, you'll save money on gas, car repairs, and those car payments.

We Americans sure do love our cars, don't we? But have you ever stopped to think about other ways to get around? Seriously, ditching the car habit is surprisingly awesome! Think of all the good things that could come from it.

Just picture this: What if cars disappeared? Wouldn't it be nice if shops were nearby? Forget those giant malls way out of town! Instead, just a quick stroll down the street. Homes, stores, and workplaces all close together, easily walkable.

Let me tell you, walking to stores rocks! It's a no-brainer: less driving. And if we all cut back on driving, guess what? More walking and biking! Hello, better health! Even science says a daily walk does wonders for your brain.

And, of course, less driving means cleaner air. Cars pump out nasty pollution that becomes smog – yuck! So, driving less? Less stress and cleaner air. Win-win!

Here's a big one: cars are huge greenhouse gas emitters! In Europe, it's already 12% of all greenhouse gases. But get this: in the US, it's a whopping 50%! Seriously, driving less makes a huge difference for the earth.

Bottom line? There are other ways to get around! We can choose options that are good for our bodies and the planet. So, with all these perks to driving less, why not give it a shot and make our lives better?

Picture a world where cars don't exist. This might seem far-fetched, yet these ideas are becoming real! New towns are now being built, some cities are limiting car access, and countries are doing car-free days. Cars cause a lot of pollution, which people often forget. We need to take action and think about the damage!

For example, Vauban, Germany, is amazing. There, you must lose your car or pay a big $40,000 parking fee! Guess what the study says? "70% of families in Vauban don't have cars, and 57% sold theirs to move there." One mom, Heidrun Walter, said, "I was always stressed with a car. Now I'm much happier!" This is "Smart Planning," designing areas to not need cars. Cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases and a huge 50% in the U.S. Smart Planning can cut these gases and clean our air!

Paris, France, shows how bad car pollution gets. One source mentioned, "After heavy pollution, Paris restricted some cars to clean the air." This cut traffic by 60% in only five days. The main cause of smog was diesel fuel, used by 67% of French vehicles. The weather helped, as "cold nights and warm days trapped car emissions," according to what was said.

It's not just Europe, though. Bogota, Colombia, had its third "Day Without Cars," only allowing buses and taxis. Carlos Arturo Plaza stated, "It's a good chance to reduce stress and pollution." Furthermore, Cali and Valledupar, two other Colombian cities, had their first Day Without Cars this year!

Car use is changing even in the U.S. Would you believe it? Miles driven peaked in 2005 and have been decreasing since. One source claims, "In April 2013, miles driven per person were almost 9% lower than the peak, like going back to January 1995." Less car use is great for the environment but bad for car companies. Ford and Mercedes are becoming "mobility" companies. New York City has bike-sharing and new bike paths. Tunnel tolls also show this change.

Cars have hurt our planet, and we have to fix it. With these awesome, eco-friendly ideas, we're going toward a future where cars don't control us.

The Electoral College? It's a head-scratcher, for sure! It's been around for ages, but honestly, the original reasons are kinda outdated. Back then, folks weren't exactly political experts, so informed voting was tough. Now? News and candidate info are everywhere! Shouldn't the popular vote decide the president? The president should answer to us all, not just the bigwigs.

Okay, even if you dig the Electoral College, issues exist. We think we're voting for president, but it's really for electors. States pick them their own way. These electors pledge to a candidate, then vote. But, as Bradford Plumer notes, you can't really control electors! Plus, he says voters get confused, messing up votes. Sometimes, "faithless electors" break promises!

Candidates only obsess over "swing states." These states are up for grabs, super important. The Electoral College hands all state votes to the winner, so a Democrat in a die-hard Republican state might skip voting; it seems pointless. Thus, campaigns ignore the rest. With a popular vote, they'd have to talk to everyone.

A popular vote for president? Way fairer! Even small groups in reliably voting states would get a say. A poll showed most voters want to pick the president directly. Even Electoral College fans admit it could cause a tie. They even say one vote doesn't matter—crazy talk! That kills voter enthusiasm and harms elections. Every vote counts, and everyone deserves representation. A popular vote? Best way to elect a president for ALL Americans. Everyone should participate. This should apply to everyone, so let's all make sure we vote!

Post-WWII, cars gained immense popularity. Sources 2 and 3 examine cities implementing car-free days, despite some individuals in sources 1 and 4 reporting reduced driving. Less driving means less pollution and easier movement.

Air pollution poses a significant problem. Rosenthal notes, "cars create 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases... and up to 50% in some U.S. places P5." Heavy car usage results in urban smog. During the 2009 Beijing Olympics, fewer cars led to noticeably improved air quality! This action was crucial to safeguard athletes and tourists from illness, given that mask-wearing was already widespread in Beijing. Although difficult, limiting car use prevents negative consequences. Duffer states that Paris has substantially lowered pollution levels, even banning certain license plates P19. Curbing car pollution ensures cleaner air and improved visibility!

Surprisingly, many young people are forgoing licenses, despite growing up with cars. Selsky claims that fewer cars mean more parks, sports fields, shops, and pedestrian zones P28. Moreover, new businesses and restaurants can flourish! Instead of highway-adjacent malls, shopping areas are emerging near public transportation and pedestrian-friendly locations. Rosenthal mentions his family's increased reliance on public transit, carpools, and walking instead of driving P40. Opting for public transit is now a savvy choice! Carpooling is also quite valuable. Thanks to carpools, walking, and buses, city navigation and exploration have become much simpler.

Cities are evolving to improve life for those without cars. Limiting car use offers numerous advantages. Cleaner air and easier transportation are diminishing the dominance of cars. Reducing car usage promises even greater quality of life improvements down the line!

Okay, so the electoral college? People are picked as electors, they vote for the president, and then those votes are tallied. But is this truly a great way to do things, or is it a disaster waiting to happen? Frankly, it needs to go. It's not a direct popular vote, and that could lead to big problems.

Some people argue that the electoral college prevents confusing runoff elections, possibly leading to smoother governance. Richard A. Posner noted that in really close elections, people actually *want* runoffs. Therefore, keeping the electoral college, they claim, reduces the chance of runoffs. But is that actually a good way to solve the problem, and what challenges might it cause? Because electors pick the president, can we even rely on them to stop runoffs? Posner believes it does reduce the temptation to make things worse. Still, that single perk isn't worth all of the serious downsides.

Imagine if everything fails entirely? We'd be in big trouble! The writer mentions that we were lucky that the 2000 election was only the worst in a century; things could certainly get a lot worse. It's happened before, too. Plumer thinks the electoral college has greatly harmed the U.S., and keeping it could result in even more mayhem. The writer reminds us that electors have, in the past, voted against their party, according to Plumer. Therefore, it's not only pointless, but also easily manipulated. It should be scrapped, since it could ruin our government.

What's more, it's not a direct popular election. The Office of the Federal Register says when you vote for a candidate, you're really voting for *their* electors. Thus, you're not directly choosing who leads the whole nation. It seems wrong for leaders to make big decisions affecting the people when they did not vote for them. Plumer calls it unfair, out-of-date, and just idiotic. These issues prove the electoral college hurts trust in government. Plumer also points out that candidates just ignore states they don't think they can win, focusing only on "swing" states. Winning has become more important than what is truly best for the nation. Will we really allow this thing to destroy us?

In short, the electoral college? Get rid of it.

Dear Florida Senator, Our country's founders made electors so the president would be who the people picked. But, the person everyone votes for might not win because of this. It's an old system, so we could end up with a tie. Lots of Americans want real democracy, but this system can mess that up.

The popular vote tells us what the country truly wants. A huge number – "over 60 percent" – want to directly vote for the president, showing they want their vote to count more. When the President sends soldiers to war, citizens want their voices to be heard and their vote to matter, not get ignored by the electoral college. Only 538 electors have a ton more power than millions of normal voters. Plus, we can't forget that we have rights like "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," and freedom goes hand in hand with voting.

The electoral college has existed for many years. However, this is a big part of the issue. If the U.S. wants to remain modern and in step, we must update how the rules work. The world changes constantly, so the system should change, which would help us all improve. We should be thankful that "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century" because it might happen again.

Even worse, we might get a tie, making things worse. As it was stated, "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Ties aren't how democracy should work. The people's vote, not gridlock, should decide things. Source 2 claims, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." The popular vote doesn't matter if there's a tie, which is very alarming.

To summarize, the electoral college should be done away with since it damages the people. The system makes our votes less important, it's old, and it could cause a tie. I hope we pick the president by what the people want, not by those in D.C.

Okay, so picture cars, right? They take you places – errands or with buddies. Now, imagine if they vanished? Weird, huh? Yet, believe it or not, some good stuff could actually happen.

First off, a massive win for our planet! Cars are super polluting, no? Listen to this: "Passenger cars? They're responsible for 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases!" And get this! "Up to half in car-crazy areas of the US!" (That's from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). So, ditch the cars, get cleaner air, and lose that nasty smog. The air would just be way better!

Also, we'd be healthier. Cleaner air, for sure! Plus, no cars means walking or biking. "Vauban," per "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, "with 5,500 folks tightly packed, is car-free." Shops are close, "easy to walk to," not "miles away." So, people walk, bike, or bus it. More exercise, less couch time. Sweet!

Furthermore, cars may be fading out. "Obama's aim to cut US emissions," claims "The End of Car Culture," paragraph 29, "is helped by people buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting fewer licenses." Gas costs a ton, texting while driving is risky, and new drivers are often clueless. People seem to be liking cars less. Great for the Earth, right? Maybe driving will totally vanish someday.

So, fewer cars? That's a total score! The Earth can breathe easy, and people are already ditching their cars. Why burn gas when you can walk, bike, and stay fit? Less driving? Awesome!

Dating back centuries, the Electoral College evokes intense emotions and constant debate. Should it be abolished, or should it remain unchanged? Some argue it's inherently unjust, harming both voters and candidates alike, while others maintain it's the optimal method for electing a president. Given the validity of both perspectives, what, if any, modifications should be implemented?

Rather than simply tallying votes, the Electoral College aims to strike a balance in presidential elections. Each state's political parties propose a slate of electors. Thus, when you cast your vote, you're essentially supporting the electors chosen by your candidate.

Why, then, is there a push to eliminate the Electoral College? Well, complications can arise. A key concern is the potential for gridlock. How might this occur? Electors might deviate and vote based on personal convictions. Although you believe your vote selects electors, they might not always align with your expectations. This poses a significant issue if electors abandon their party's nominee. Following Al Gore's popular vote victory in 2000, which did not translate into electoral victory, many voters advocated for direct elections. A significant segment of the electorate strongly dislikes the Electoral College.

However, some staunchly defend its continued existence. Each party selects electors likely to support their nominee, and typically, they do. The party tends to select individuals who will uphold their candidate. It also underscores the importance of securing support across diverse regions of the nation. A president who only wins in a small area may not be qualified to lead everyone. They are unable to assist everyone if they only prioritize one location. As a result, they probably won't win. Also, the Electoral College can assist when no individual secures a definitive majority.

In deliberating the fate of the Electoral College—whether to preserve or dismantle it—compelling arguments exist on both sides. Ultimately, the decision rests with the populace.

Cars are a must for parents needing to shuttle kids to school and stuff. Yet, more and more people are thinking maybe driving less could help fix some of the messes we're in.

Everyone knows cars mess up the planet. Air pollution and greenhouse gases that crank up global warming are big worries. In Europe, cars cause about 12% of these nasty emissions. But get this: in the US, where everyone drives all the time, it can be as much as 50%! Plus, crazy traffic makes it all worse. Take France – diesel cars make city smog even thicker, especially when it's hot. So, driving less means cleaner air for everyone.

Besides helping the Earth, ditching the car can make you feel better too. Lots of folks around the world have tried living without a car, picking other ways to get around. Heidrun Walkter, a German mom with money, dumped her car and now loves walking and hearing bikes and kids. "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I'm much happier," she says. Also, less driving pushes you to move more. In Colombia, millions went car-free for a day, walking, biking, or taking the bus to fight smog. It worked! It made people feel closer and less stressed. One guy biking with his wife said it was good for the Earth and for them.

So, driving less is an easy way to clean up the air, cut down smog everywhere, and get more bikes on the road. From Germany to France, Colombia, and the US, tons of countries are trying to find ways to deal with the bad stuff that comes from driving.

Senator, back the plan to pick Presidents by who gets the most votes! It's super important, and here's the deal. Electors might just do their own thing, not what folks want. Plus, we could get a tie, and then the House picks. And let's be real, winner-take-all stinks.

Electors aren't always reliable, and they could mess up again. Like Bradford Plumer said, some almost ruined the 1960 election by switching electors. Sometimes they just vote how they feel, even against their own party!

A tie in the Electoral College? Yikes! Then the House decides, but each state gets one vote. Get this: tiny Wyoming has the same say as huge California! In 1976, just a few votes changing in Ohio and Hawaii could've made a tie. That was way too close for comfort!

Bottom line, the winner-take-all setup is just not fair to voters. Candidates only care about swing states, ignoring everyone else. Back in 2000, tons of states got zero love from candidates. Many city voters didn't even see an ad. How can we let a few voters in places like Ohio pick the President? It's tough to justify.

So, Senator, listen up: back a popular vote system for picking Presidents! Don't forget, electors could ditch the voters, a tie sends it to the House, and winner-take-all is super unfair and not the right thing for America.
